gmw_00005601.name: WISEJ104939.34+154837.8
gmw_00005601.overview: WISEJ104939.34+154837.8 is a gamma-ray blazar candidate [arXiv:1404.5211] located at a redshift of z = 0.3271 [arXiv:1711.05476]. Its optical spectrum is almost featureless [arXiv:1404.5211], displaying Ca II absorption lines [arXiv:1711.05476, arXiv:1404.5211] and possible faint [OII] emission [arXiv:1711.05476].

sources.summary: The paper resolves conflicting redshifts for 3FGLJ1049.7+1548, confirming z = 0.3271 from a higher-S/N spectrum and detecting Ca II absorption plus possible faint [OII] emission.

quotes.quote: 3FGLJ1049.7+1548 : In the 3FGL this object is classified as UGS. However, the source was associated to the optical counterpart SDSS J104939.35+154837.6 by paggi2014 who propose a redshift z = 0.327 based on Ca II (3934 , 3968 ) absorption lines. On the other hand a better quality optical spectrum was obtained by the SDSS Boss survey suggesting a redshift z = 1.452. Given the large inconsistency we obtain an high SNR spectrum for this object that confirms the redshift of z = 0.3271 (see Fig. fig:spectra and Fig. fig:spectraCU ). In addition to Ca II absorption doublet, we detect a possible faint [ OII ] emission.

sources.summary: The paper classifies WISEJ104939.34+154837.8 as a radio-and-infrared selected gamma-ray blazar candidate, with an almost featureless BZB-compatible spectrum and redshift estimate z = 0.33.

quotes.quote: Likewise, three optical spectra of 1800 s each of source WISEJ104939.34+154837.8 were secured with the 2.1-meter telescope of the Observatorio Astron omico Nacional (OAN) in San Pedro M artir (M exico) on 2 May 2013 with mid-exposure time 04:58 UT. In addition, optical spectroscopy can be used to test the predictions of the different association procedure that are used to find ray blazar candidates lying in the uncertainty regions at 95 level of confidence of UGSs or AGUs listed in the 2FGL or 2LAC . In particular the sources WISEJ050558.78+611335.9 and WISEJ064459.38+603131.7 are selected by 2013arXiv1303.3585M as class C ray blazar candidates of BZB and undefined type, respectively; WISEJ060102.86+383829.2 is selected as ray blazar candidate by massaro2013b on the basis of its low-frequency radio properties; WISEJ022051.24+250927.6 is selected as ray blazar candidate by 2013ApJS..209....9P combining its Swift X-ray emission and its IR WISE colors; finally, WISEJ104939.34+154837.8 is selected as ray blazar candidate by massaro2013c combining its radio emission and its IR WISE colors.

In particular, WISEJ050558.78+611335.9 and WISEJ060102.86+383829.2 show featureless BZB spectra, while WISEJ104939.34+154837.8 shows an almost featureless spectrum with weak absorption lines consistent with Ca ii H K yielding a redshift estimate z = 0.33 ; the EW of these line - 0.7  - is however consistent with the BZB definition given in Sect. sec:intro .

gmw_00005602.name: NGC 633
gmw_00005602.overview: NGC 633 is an M51-type galaxy system featuring a main galaxy with two well-developed arms [arXiv:1605.02842]. This system exhibits a disturbed tidal structure and mild kinematic asymmetries, with hydrogen emission detected in both nuclei [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 633 as an M51-type system with developed arms, disturbed tidal structure, H emission in both nuclei, and mild kinematic asymmetries in the main galaxy.

quotes.quote: NGC 633 In the DSS picture, the main galaxy presents two well-developed arms. Both arms end in a diffuse fashion, near the satellite galaxy, so it would be possible to consider this object as a not typical M51-type system, taking into account that it is the shorter arm that is closer in projection to the satellite galaxy. Apparently, the satellite would be the cause of the longer extension that presents the longer tidal arm. This tidal arm seems to begin in the south-western part of the disk of the main galaxy, although it is difficult to locate exactly in which part of the disk it originates, because of its very diffuse structure.

gmw_00005603.name: ARP 54
gmw_00005603.overview: ARP 54 is an interacting M51-type system featuring a main galaxy with an ocular SABc classification [arXiv:1605.02842]. The main galaxy and its satellite companion exhibit a radial velocity difference of 60 km/s [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper classifies ARP 54 as an ocular SABc M51-type system, measures a 60 km/s component velocity difference, and finds only slight kinematic asymmetry from the satellite.

quotes.quote: ARP 54 The main galaxy is classified as SABc and presents an ``ocular" morphology, a denomination that makes reference to the apparent shape of the ring of the galaxy and the nuclear region. The difference in radial velocity between both components is of 60 km s ^ 1 (the satellite radial velocity had no previous determinations and have been performed in CASLEO with the same instrumental configuration described above, but with a 300 lines mm ^ 1 grating). The arm that would connect with the satellite abruptly interrupts, in both 2MASS and in DSS images. The radial velocity distribution along PA 90 ^ for the main galaxy was measured from very faint H emission in its central 6", so it was not possible to derive kinematic information for the central region.

gmw_00005604.name: AM 0327-285
gmw_00005604.overview: AM 0327-285 is an interacting galaxy system characterized by a relatively small separation between its components [arXiv:1605.02842]. The system exhibits radial-velocity asymmetries, including a velocity rise toward the satellite galaxy and a decrease of about 100 km/s to the southwest [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper reports that AM 0327-285 has closely separated components and radial-velocity asymmetries, including a rise toward the satellite and variations linked to spiral arms.

quotes.quote: AM 0327-285 This system presents a relatively small separation between the components. In the direction that connects both objects, the distribution has an observed maximum of 250 km s ^ 1 with some slight asymmetries, as a rise in the radial velocity toward the direction of the satellite. The perturbation perhaps is more notorious along PA 30 ^ , where the peak of the continuum emission coincides with the position that belongs to the maximum in radial velocity. From this position, to the SW, the curve decreases about 100 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00005605.name: AM 0403-604
gmw_00005605.overview: AM 0403-604 is an M51-type interacting system featuring a bridge that directly connects the central region of the main galaxy to its companion [arXiv:1605.02842]. The main galaxy hosts a Seyfert 2 active nucleus, and the system exhibits a high velocity difference of 490 km/s between the two galaxies [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper describes AM 0403-604 as an M51-type system with a bridge, a Seyfert 2 main galaxy, a large component velocity difference, and a direct central connection to its companion.

quotes.quote: AM 0403-604 In this system, besides its M51-type nature, a bridge is apparently connecting both galaxies. The main galaxy has a nuclear emission corresponding to Seyfert 2 type (as determined from spectrophotometric observations performed at CASLEO in October 2007). The velocity difference between main and satellite galaxy, which had no previous determinations, is rather high, being of 490 km s ^ 1 . It is suggestive that one of two active objects of the sample presents a direct connection (bridge) between the central region of the galaxy and its companion.

gmw_00005606.name: AM 0458-250
gmw_00005606.overview: AM 0458-250 is a large, massive spiral galaxy with a diameter of approximately 50 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1605.02842]. It forms a confirmed physical pair with its satellite galaxy, which displays no distortions in its velocity distribution [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper describes AM 0458-250 as a large massive spiral M51-type system, confirms its satellite as a physical pair, and notes no significant velocity distortions.

quotes.quote: AM 0458-250 The main galaxy of this system is large (according to LEDA data, its 25 mag/arcsec ^ 2 diameter in B-band is of 1.15' or about 50 kpc). The main galaxy is a massive spiral (the mass may be estimated from the velocity distribution, in a Keplerian approximation). Its satellite had no previous velocity determination and they are confirmed as a physical pair though no significant distortions are seen in the velocity distribution.

gmw_00005607.name: AM 0459-340
gmw_00005607.overview: AM 0459-340 is an interacting system where the main galaxy forms a physical pair with a confirmed satellite galaxy [arXiv:1605.02842]. The main galaxy exhibits a highly asymmetric radial velocity curve along its major axis, while the satellite displays a rigid body curve across most of its extension [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper finds AM 0459-340 has a highly asymmetric main-galaxy radial velocity curve, a confirmed physical satellite, and a bridge region with enhanced radial-velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: AM 0459-340 The heliocentric radial velocity curve, along the major axis of the main galaxy, presents high asymmetry respect to the position of the nuclear region. The observed radial velocity gradient of the NE branch is of 35 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 , reaching a maximum 10" from the center, and with a difference of 80 km s ^ 1 with respect to that of the central part. Here we find that the satellite forms a physical pair with the main galaxy. The satellite depicts a rigid body curve, in most of its extension.

gmw_00005608.name: AM 0639-582
gmw_00005608.overview: AM 0639-582 is an interacting system consisting of a spiral main galaxy and an irregular satellite galaxy [arXiv:1605.02842]. A long tidal tail extends between the two galaxies, which interact in a prograde orbit [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper describes AM 0639-582 as a system with a long tidal tail, an irregular satellite, prograde orbit, and differing radial-velocity gradients across the main galaxy.

quotes.quote: AM 0639-582 This system has a long tidal tail, which extends from the spiral main galaxy to an irregular galaxy. The radial velocity distribution along the main galaxy major axis is apparently normal, but there is a well-marked difference in the observed gradients, being the gradient more pronounced in the satellite direction, between 4" north from the center and 3" to the south, while the gradient is lower between 4" and 18" to the south. Observations along the centers of both galaxies were also made, determining that the orbit is prograde. The satellite radial velocity is similar to the velocity that corresponds to the NW branch of the main galaxy radial velocity distribution.

gmw_00005609.name: VV 410
gmw_00005609.overview: VV 410 is an interacting system consisting of a main galaxy and a satellite galaxy [arXiv:1605.02842]. The main galaxy displays a velocity distribution along its bar orientation, while the nearly edge-on satellite galaxy exhibits a rigid-body velocity distribution [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper reports radial-velocity distributions for both VV 410 components, describing a rigid-body satellite curve and a spiral-like main-galaxy curve most perturbed toward the satellite.

quotes.quote: VV 410 The radial velocity distributions have been obtained for both objects along their main axis. The satellite galaxy has a rigid body velocity distribution along almost the whole main body. Considering that the smaller galaxy is nearly edge-on, it is not possible to assure if the radial velocity curve shape obeys a matter distribution dominated by a spheroidal component, or if it is a consequence of dust opacity (see D iaz et al. 2001 for a similar case). The main galaxy shows a velocity distribution, along the bar orientation, with a velocity amplitude and aspect typical of spiral galaxies of this morphology type.

gmw_00005610.name: VV 350
gmw_00005610.overview: VV 350 is an interacting system composed of a nearly edge-on SABb main galaxy and an edge-on SBb satellite galaxy [arXiv:1605.02842]. The main galaxy's disk is well-defined opposite the satellite, but appears diffuse and perturbed in the region closer to its companion [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper describes VV 350 as an edge-on SABb/SBb pair with new kinematics, a perturbed side near the companion, and asymmetries in the satellite velocity distribution.

quotes.quote: VV 350 This system is conformed by a main galaxy of SABb type, nearly edge-on, and a satellite SBb, also edge-on, and no kinematic information was available till present. We obtained spectra for the major axis of both galaxies. It is interesting to notice how the disk of the main galaxy (seen in optical images) is better defined in the zone opposite to the satellite, while the region nearer to the companion appears diffuse or perturbed, perhaps by effect of the interaction. Opposite to the direction to the satellite, the H emission is higher than in the other direction.

gmw_00005611.name: AM 1304-333
gmw_00005611.overview: AM 1304-333 is an interacting galaxy system that represents an extreme M51-type case [arXiv:1605.02842]. It features a perturbed disk with plumes, a knotty central structure, and a nucleus that is displaced from its geometrical center [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper treats AM 1304-333 as an extreme M51-type case with displaced nucleus, knotty central structure, perturbed disk, plumes, and strong kinematic perturbations.

quotes.quote: AM 1304-333 It is clearly seen in the DSS images that the nuclei of the main galaxy is apparently displaced with respect to the geometrical center of the main galaxy disk. In 2010 we obtained broad band images with the Swope 1 m telescope of Las Campanas Observatory. These shows that the central region is conformed by a nest of bright knots. This system seems to be an extreme case, in mass-ratio, morphology and kinematic perturbations.

gmw_00005612.name: AM 2256-304
gmw_00005612.overview: AM 2256-304 is an interacting galaxy system that may represent an extreme case of a high-velocity encounter [arXiv:1605.02842]. The two galaxies exhibit a large systemic radial velocity difference of 1400 km/s, and the main galaxy displays non-circular motions likely caused by its spiral arms [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper considers AM 2256-304 a possible high-velocity encounter, with a large systemic velocity difference, symmetric main-galaxy curve, oscillations, and spiral-arm non-circular motions.

quotes.quote: AM 2256-304 This system could be an extreme case of a high velocity encounter. The systemic radial velocity difference between both galaxies is 1400 km s ^ 1 . The velocity distribution for the main galaxy is symmetric, presenting oscillations in the flat region of the curve, of 50 km s ^ 1 in the north and reaching 8 km s ^ 1 in the southern region, where originates the tidal arm that reaches the apparent position of the satellite. This non-circular motions are probably consequence of the presence of spiral arms.

gmw_00005613.name: AM 0430-285
gmw_00005613.overview: AM 0430-285 is an interacting M51-type galaxy system consisting of a main galaxy and a satellite [arXiv:1605.02842]. The satellite galaxy exhibits rotation with an amplitude of 60 km/s and is connected to a small condensation by a faint bridge or arm [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper confirms AM 0430-285 as a physical M51-type pair, describing a bridge or arm to the satellite, satellite rotation, a prograde orbit, and asymmetric main-galaxy velocities.

quotes.quote: AM 0430-285 The morphology of this system is similar to NGC 646, an M51 system which is not included in this work, but observed in CASLEO for a spectrophotometric study. A small condensation joined by a faint bridge/arm with the satellite galaxy is observed. This object did not have previous radial velocity measurements, the present work confirms that it constitutes a physical pair with the main galaxy. The direction which connects the center of main and satellite galaxies, coincides with the major axis of the satellite, which presents rotation, with an amplitude of 60 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00005614.name: AM 1325-274
gmw_00005614.overview: AM 1325-274 is an interacting system featuring a main galaxy that appears to be oriented nearly face-on [arXiv:1605.02842]. The velocity distribution of this main galaxy shows a steady decrease with oscillations, while its satellite companion has a velocity that continues the main rotation curve [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper says AM 1325-274 has a nearly face-on main galaxy, a PA 166 velocity distribution with oscillations, and a newly measured satellite velocity continuous with the main rotation curve.

quotes.quote: AM 1325-274 The main galaxy seems to be quite face on. The velocity distribution at PA 166 ^ has a steady decrease with oscillations of 40 km s ^ 1 . The velocity of the satellite (which had no previous determinations) seems to be a continuation of the main galaxy rotation curve.

gmw_00005615.name: AM 2105-332
gmw_00005615.overview: AM 2105-332 is an interacting galaxy that exhibits central hydrogen emission spanning over 5 kpc [arXiv:1605.02842]. The galaxy displays a large rotation amplitude of 360 km/s, with solid-body rotation occurring within its central 2 kpc [arXiv:1605.02842].

sources.summary: The paper describes AM 2105-332 as having central H emission over 5 kpc, a large rotation amplitude, mostly solid-body central rotation, and mild outer asymmetry toward the satellite.

quotes.quote: AM 2105-332 For the main galaxy it is only possible to detect H emission for the central 5 kpc. The amplitude of the rotation curve is 360 km s ^ 1 . The extension from which we can derive information is too small compared with the extension of the galaxy. Apparently, there is solid-body rotation in the central 2 kpc.

gmw_00005616.name: NGC 5106
gmw_00005616.overview: NGC 5106 is a radio-loud galaxy classified as a LINER, though it exhibits contradicting classification indicators [arXiv:1803.06877]. The galaxy features an extended ultraviolet nucleus, a high star formation rate exceeding 20 solar masses per year, and a high millimeter-to-X-ray ratio that may indicate a compact radio jet [arXiv:1803.06877].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5106 as a radio-loud LINER outlier with the sample’s highest 100 GHz flux, elevated millimeter-to-X-ray ratio, ambiguous classification, and possible compact jet.

quotes.quote: The highest flux of 24.1 mJy is measured for NGC 5106, which is the (only) LINER in the sample (Table obslog ), and will be discussed in more detail in Sec. The one outlier is the suspected LINER NGC 5106 at the very top of Figs. NGC 5106 is radio loud in terms of its 5 GHz to B-band flux density ratio, and its value of (L_ mm / L_ X ) = -2.37 is high compared to the present X-ray selected sample, though not unusual for low-luminosity AGN .f., doi11, Wu18 .

Parisi14 point to contradicting indicators regarding the classification of NGC 5106 (PBC J1321.1+0858 there). Indeed, NGC 5106 has a 20 extended UV nucleus, and its FIR emission indicates an unusually high star formation rate of above 20 solar masses per year, one of the three highest of the \ AGN sample Melendez14 . We did not find any high-resolution radio images of NGC 5106, but its high L_ mm / L_ X ratio could be explained if it has a compact radio jet.

gmw_00005617.name: PJ014341.2
gmw_00005617.overview: PJ014341.2 is a dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.095 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Observations reveal a compact galaxy-galaxy lensing system, where a foreground galaxy at z = 0.594 creates a partially complete Einstein ring [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ014341.2 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ014341.2: A photometric redshift support from the combined SED analysis of the , \ and ALMA measurements suggests that the single line detected by the RSR measurement corresponds to a CO (2--1) line emission at a source redshift, z = 1.095 . The ALMA 260 GHz observation reveals a compact galaxy-galaxy lensing system with a partially complete Einstein ring with a diameter of 1.5 . The foreground lensing SDSS galaxy has a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.594 .

gmw_00005618.name: PJ022634.0
gmw_00005618.overview: PJ022634.0 is a dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.120 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Hubble Space Telescope imaging reveals that the galaxy is gravitationally lensed into an Einstein ring with a diameter of 3 arcseconds around a foreground elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ022634.0 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ022634.0: With a \ detection of S_ 350 1800 mJy, this system is the brightest in our sample. Its apparent SED peaking in the \ 350 \ band suggests this to be a low redshift ( z 2 ) source, but our subsequent spectroscopic follow-up observations by harrington20 have shown that the single line detected by the RSR is a CO (3--2) line at z = 3.120 . The HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image (Lowenthal et al., in prep) shows an Einstein ring with a diameter of 3 centred on a large elliptical galaxy with a photometric redshift of z=0.41 wen15 .

gmw_00005619.name: PJ030510.6
gmw_00005619.overview: PJ030510.6 is a dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.263 [arXiv:2206.00138]. High-resolution ALMA observations resolve the source into two compact peaks, which correspond to offset stellar components imaged by the Hubble Space Telescope [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ030510.6 as a dusty galaxy source with CO redshift constraints and far-infrared/submillimeter measurements from RSR, Herschel, AzTEC, or ALMA.

quotes.quote: PJ030510.6: The derived source redshift, z = 2.263 , is based on the CO (3--2) line detection with the RSR, and this is confirmed by our follow-up spectroscopy harrington20 . It is detected in two \ bands, and ALMA 260 GHz measurements recover the total flux density measured by AzTEC 1.1 mm observations. The ALMA resolves the source into two separate and compact peaks with a less than 1 separation. The HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image shows one stellar component well centred on the brighter 260 GHz peak while the second stellar component is clearly offset from the second 260 GHz peak.

gmw_00005620.name: PJ105322.6
gmw_00005620.overview: PJ105322.6 is a dusty, high-redshift starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.549 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It is gravitationally lensed by a compact group of red galaxies at z=0.837, which creates a nearly complete Einstein ring [arXiv:2206.00138], and it appears to be extremely gas-rich [arXiv:1811.11215].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ105322.6 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ105322.6: The two CO lines detected in the RSR spectrum leads to an unambiguous redshift of z=3.549 for this 857 GHz drop-out \ source. This is the brightest 1.1 mm sources detected with AzTEC ( 202 30 mJy) and has a fully mapped rest frame far-IR SED because this is one of the \ sources studied using \ (PLCK G145.2+50.9). The SMA 850 \ continuum imaging by canameras15 shows a nearly complete Einstein ring with 5 radius. Using their \ F110W and F160W band imaging and ground-based spectroscopy, frye19 have shown that the lensing mass is a small compact group of red galaxies at z=0.837 .

sources.summary: PLCK G145.2+50.9 has a robust extra spectral component but only two detected CO transitions, limiting detailed gas-property constraints, and appears especially gas-rich.

quotes.quote: Seven out of the 11 GEMS exhibit single Gaussian profiles and we only use a double Gaussian for PLCK G045.1+61.1, PLCK G092.5+42.9, PLCK G145.2+50.9, and PLCK G244.8+54.9, where this additional spectral component is robustly detected (see Figures fig:lines1 , fig:lines2 , fig:lines3 , and fig:lines4 ).

For this reason, the only two CO transitions detected in PLCK G145.2+50.9 and the flat ladder of PLCK G113.7+61.0 in the range J_ up =4 -8 will, to a certain extent, prevent us from deriving detailed gas properties in these sources.

PLCK G145.2+50.9 is almost a factor two more massive than other sources in the sample, suggesting that this is an extremely gas-rich starburst or that our estimate of the magnification factor may be too low, for example due to the presence of large-scale structures at different redshifts along the line of sight.

gmw_00005621.name: PJ112713.4
gmw_00005621.overview: PJ112713.4 is a dusty starburst galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of z = 1.303 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Hubble Space Telescope imaging reveals an Einstein ring with a diameter of 1 arcsecond around a foreground galaxy, indicating a galaxy-galaxy gravitational lensing system [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ112713.4 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ112713.4: This system has only a single \ detection, with S_ 350 200 mJy, and was not observed by AzTEC or by ALMA. The single line detected by the RSR observations is tentatively interpreted as a CO (2--1) line at z = 1.303 , and this is later confirmed by our spectroscopic follow-up observations harrington20 . The HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image shows an Einstein ring with 1 diameter centred on a compact galaxy with an SDSS photometric redshift of z=0.42 , suggesting a galaxy-galaxy lensing (Lowenthal et al., in prep).

gmw_00005622.name: PJ113805.5
gmw_00005622.overview: PJ113805.5 is a dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.019 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It is gravitationally lensed by a foreground elliptical galaxy, which creates a compact, elongated structure visible in high-resolution continuum imaging [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ113805.5 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ113805.5: The single line detected by the RSR is interpreted to be a CO (2--1) line at a redshift of z = 2.019 based on photometric support using the SED analysis, and this redshift is confirmed by our spectroscopic follow-up observations harrington20 . This source is detected in all 3 \ bands and by ALMA, with S_ 350 200 mJy. It is likely lensed by a single foreground elliptical galaxy with an SDSS photometric redshift of z=0.52 , as the ALMA 260 GHz continuum image shows a compact ( 1.5 ) elongated structure centred on the optical galaxy seen in the HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image.

gmw_00005623.name: PJ113921.7
gmw_00005623.overview: PJ113921.7 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy with a CO line redshift of z=2.858 [arXiv:2206.00138]. ALMA observations of the source reveal a compact ring of emission that is gravitationally lensed around a central galaxy [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ113921.7 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ113921.7: PJ113921.7 is another high-redshift \ source studied with a \ follow-up study (PLCK G231.3+72.2) that we independently recovered from the PCCS using our \ colour selection and AzTEC photometry. The CO (3--2) line redshift of z=2.858 is derived from our RSR observations and the photometric support, and this redshift agrees well with those measured by other multi-line studies canameras18,nesvadba19,harrington20 . Our ALMA 260 GHz continuum image shows a compact ring of emission with 1.5 diameter, centred around a compact galaxy seen in our HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image (Lowenthal et al., in prep). No visible counterpart to the ALMA 260 GHz continuum emission is seen in the HST image, and the redshift of the lensing galaxy is unknown.

gmw_00005624.name: PJ114038.5
gmw_00005624.overview: PJ114038.5 is an infrared bright galaxy [arXiv:2206.00138] with a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.1652 [arXiv:2206.00138]. In SDSS images, it displays a compact red core surrounded by a blue irregular structure, which is characteristic of a recent merger system [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PJ114038.5 as a foreground low-redshift galaxy using SDSS/RSR evidence, describing its morphology, emission-line redshift, and radio or infrared properties.

quotes.quote: PJ114038.5: The colour-selected \ source J114038.5+53215 associated with this PCCS object shows a compact red core surrounded by a blue irregular structure, characteristic of a recent merger system in the SDSS images. It was previously identified as one of the infrared bright galaxies found by the Infrared Astronomical Satellite (``IRAS 2Jy Sample"), with a redshift of z=0.092 strauss92 . The SDSS database offers no spectroscopic data, but it reports a photometric redshift of 0.127 0.029 . Our RSR spectrum shows a single emission line at 98.932 GHz, and this corresponds to a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.1652 0.0001 if the line is interpreted as that of CO (1--0) transition.

gmw_00005625.name: PJ114329.5
gmw_00005625.overview: PJ114329.5 is a foreground, low-redshift galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.2118 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It exhibits a red color, a smooth morphology, and a spectrum with strong emission lines typical of a star-forming galaxy [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PJ114329.5 as a foreground low-redshift galaxy using SDSS/RSR evidence, describing its morphology, emission-line redshift, and radio or infrared properties.

quotes.quote: PJ114329.5: The colour-selected \ source J114329.5+680107 associated with this PCCS object is associated with a well resolved SDSS galaxy with red colour and a smooth morphology. The SDSS spectrum shows strong emission lines and bright continuum, typical of a star forming galaxy at z=0.212 . Our RSR spectrum shows a single emission line at 95.124 GHz, and this corresponds to a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.2118 0.0001 assuming the line is that of CO (1--0) transition. Therefore, we conclude that PJ114329.5 is another example of a foreground low-z galaxy.

gmw_00005626.name: PJ132217.5
gmw_00005626.overview: PJ132217.5 is a dusty starburst galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.068 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It is gravitationally lensed by multiple foreground galaxies, displaying a stellar Einstein ring nearly 20 arcseconds in diameter and multiple continuum clumps [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ132217.5 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ132217.5: The single CO (2--1) line detected with the RSR yields a redshift of z = 2.068 with the photometric redshift support, and this is confirmed by our spectroscopic follow-up observations harrington20 . The unresolved \ detection peaks at S_ 350 700 mJy, and the 260 GHz continuum is resolved into multiple clumps with the 0.4 resolution ALMA image. The lensed source morphology detected by ALMA (also a stellar Einstein ring detected in the HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image) is nearly 20 in diameter, and some of the 260 GHz flux might have been resolved out by ALMA, given its large angular extent. Multiple foreground galaxies are seen in the HST/WFC3 image, and a group potential might be responsible for the lensing (Lowenthal et al., in prep).

gmw_00005627.name: PJ132630.3
gmw_00005627.overview: PJ132630.3 is a high-redshift, dusty background galaxy [arXiv:1801.01831, arXiv:2206.00138] located at a redshift of z = 2.951 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It is gravitationally lensed by a foreground galaxy at redshift 0.786, which splits the source into two distinct images [arXiv:1801.01831, arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes HATLASJ132630.1+334410 as a background galaxy lensed into two non-arc images, with geometry away from the tangential caustic that makes modelling degenerate yet consistent with B13.

quotes.quote: HATLASJ132630.1+334410 : The background galaxy is lensed into two images, separated by 3.5^ , none of them resembling an arc. This suggests that the source is not lying on top of the tangential caustic, but away from it, although still inside the radial caustic to account for the presence of two images. As revealed by HST /WFC3 observations (see N17, their Fig. 3), the lens is located close to the southernmost lensed image. The lack of extended structures, like arcs or rings, makes the lens modelling more prone to degeneracies. Despite that, we find a good agreement with the results of B13.

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ132630.3 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ132630.3: PJ132630.3 is a \ high-redshift candidate source that is found in the ATLAS survey area, and it is one of the faintest \ sources detected in just 2 bands, with S_ 350 300 mJy. The single CO (3--2) line detected with the RSR yields a redshift of z = 2.951 with the photometric redshift support, and this redshift is confirmed by subsequent multi-line observations yang17,harrington20 . This source is also identified as one of the brightest \ source found in the H-ATLAS survey with the same redshift, and bussmann13 derived a lensing model using their SMA 340 GHz continuum image and the lensing galaxy redshift of 0.786, with a derived magnification factor of =4.1 0.3 for the 340 GHz continuum. Our higher resolution ALMA 260 GHz image shows a similar morphology as their SMA 340 GHz image, which is two images straddling the central lensing galaxy.

gmw_00005628.name: PJ133634.9
gmw_00005628.overview: PJ133634.9 is a high-redshift, dusty starburst galaxy at a redshift of z=3.254 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Hubble Space Telescope imaging reveals a pair of compact foreground galaxies at z=0.26 that are surrounded by a ring of nebulosity [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ133634.9 as a dusty galaxy source with CO redshift constraints and far-infrared/submillimeter measurements from RSR, Herschel, AzTEC, or ALMA.

quotes.quote: PJ133634.9: The two bright CO lines detected by the RSR are CO (3--2) and CO (4--3) lines at z=3.254 , and this interpretation is further supported by our spectroscopic follow-up observations harrington20 . It is detected in all 3 \ bands and by AzTEC, with S_ 350 650 mJy. The HST/WFC3 1.6 \ image reveals a pair of compact galaxies with an SDSS photometric redshift of z=0.26 , surrounded by a ring of nebulosity with 1 diameter (Lowenthal et al., in prep).

gmw_00005629.name: PJ141230.5
gmw_00005629.overview: PJ141230.5 is a high-redshift, dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.310 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It is gravitationally lensed by a foreground source, likely a galaxy group or over-density of red galaxies at z=0.588 [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ141230.5 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ141230.5: The two bright lines detected by the RSR are interpreted as CO (3--2) and CO (4--3) lines at z=3.310 , and this conclusion is further supported by the SED analysis for the measured photometry in the 3 \ bands as well as the \ and VLA/FIRST 1.4 GHz photometry (see Fig. fig:SEDs ). The foreground (likely lensing) SDSS galaxy has only a photometric redshift of 0.54 0.07 . However, a clear over-density of similarly red galaxies is seen in the SDSS image centred on the \ source position, including two galaxies with a SDSS spectroscopic redshift of z=0.588 within a 30 radius, and there is a high likelihood that the foreground lensing source is also at z=0.588 .

gmw_00005630.name: PJ144653.2
gmw_00005630.overview: PJ144653.2 is a dusty starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.084 [arXiv:2206.00138]. It is gravitationally lensed by a foreground late-type galaxy at z = 0.493, which is visible in Hubble Space Telescope imaging [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ144653.2 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ144653.2: This system has , AzTEC and ALMA measurements of the dust SED, with a peak \ detection of S_ 350 1150 mJy. Given its brightness in the rest frame far-IR, the single line detected by the RSR was initially thought to be a CO line at z 2 . However, our follow-up spectroscopy has shown that the RSR detection is of the CO (2--1) line at z = 1.084 harrington20 . The HST/WFC3 image shows a late type galaxy with an SDSS spectroscopic redshift of z=0.493 .

gmw_00005631.name: PJ144958.6
gmw_00005631.overview: PJ144958.6 is a lensed high-redshift starburst galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.153 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Observations reveal a 10-arcsecond-long lensed arc where stellar light and dust continuum emission trace a likely multiple system [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ144958.6 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ144958.6: The RSR detected CO (3--2) line corresponds to a source redshift of z = 2.153 , and this is confirmed by our spectroscopic follow-up observations harrington20 . The \ detection peaks at S_ 350 700 mJy, and the ALMA 260 GHz continuum observations have recovered the complete flux density of the AzTEC 1.1 mm measurements. The stellar light in the HST/WFC3 image and the ALMA 260 GHz continuum both trace the same 10 long lensed arc, with some notable differences -- only parts of the stellar arc are associated with 260 GHz continuum. Therefore, this source is clearly spatially resolved by lensing and is likely a multiple system.

gmw_00005632.name: PJ154432.4
gmw_00005632.overview: PJ154432.4 is a high-redshift, dusty galaxy [arXiv:2206.00138] with a CO line redshift of z=2.5989 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Its 1.1 mm continuum morphology suggests that the galaxy is gravitationally lensed by a foreground potential with a mass exceeding 10^13 solar masses [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ154432.4 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ154432.4: PJ154432.4 is another high-redshift \ source studied with a \ follow-up study (PLCK G080.2+49.8) that we independently recovered from the PCCS using our \ colour selection and AzTEC photometry. The CO (3--2) line redshift of z=2.5989 0.0003 derived from our RSR observations and the photometric support agrees well with the redshift reported by `` z_ source =2.6 " canameras15 . The extension and morphology of the 1.1 mm continuum imaged by AzTEC instrument (see Figure fig:AzTEC ) matches the higher resolution SMA 850 m image by Ca n ameras et al. and suggests lensing by a foreground potential with a mass exceeding 10^ 13 M_ .

gmw_00005633.name: PJ231356.6
gmw_00005633.overview: PJ231356.6 is a dusty star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.215 [arXiv:2206.00138]. Hubble Space Telescope imaging reveals that it consists of multiple gravitationally lensed objects centered around a foreground elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2206.00138].

sources.summary: The paper describes PJ231356.6 as a dusty galaxy source with CO/photometric redshift constraints and lensing morphology seen in ALMA, HST, SMA, or AzTEC data.

quotes.quote: PJ231356.6: The bright line detected by the RSR is interpreted as the CO (3--2) line at z = 2.215 , and this is confirmed by our spectroscopic follow-up harrington20 . It was detected in two \ bands and was imaged by our ALMA 260 GHz continuum observations. PJ231356.6 is located in the Atacama Cosmology Telescope (ACT) Equatorial Survey field and is identified as one of the 30 brightest DSFGs with computed deboosted flux density of S_ 148GHz =3.2^ +1.7 _ 1.4 mJy, S_ 218GHz =16.8^ +3.8 _ 4.4 mJy, S_ 277GHz =26.8^ +5.3 _ 5.3 mJy gralla20 . The HST/WFC3 image shows possibly multiple lensed objects, only some associated with the ALMA 260 GHz continuum, centred around a large elliptical galaxy with an SDSS spectroscopic redshift of z=0.560 .

gmw_00005634.name: KUG 0203-Dw1
gmw_00005634.overview: KUG 0203-Dw1 is a dwarf companion galaxy to the primary group member KUG 0203-100 [arXiv:2401.01931]. It exhibits blue colors [arXiv:2401.01931], hosts eight globular clusters [arXiv:2401.01931], and shows evidence of active gas stripping by its host [arXiv:2401.01931].

sources.summary: The paper finds KUG 0203-Dw1 has a typical dwarf-like globular cluster abundance, clear H I detection and stripping, blue colors, UV emission, and tidal connection to its host.

quotes.quote: Using Hubble Space Telescope Advanced Camera for Surveys F555W and F814W imaging, we identify 8 2 globular clusters (GCs) in KUG 0203-Dw1 and 6 2 in KDG 013, abundances that are fairly typical for normal dwarf galaxies of similar stellar mass. Jansky Very Large Array data reveal a clear \ detection of KUG 0203-Dw1 with a gas mass estimate of M_ \ /M_ 7.4 and evidence of active stripping by the host. \ gas is found near the location of KDG 013 but is likely unrelated to the UDG itself due to the morphology and the numerous gas tails within the host group.

KUG 0203-100: This galaxy is the primary group member, with KUG 0203-Dw1 as the closest companion in addition to 3 other small galaxies. KUG 0203-100 (also known as SDSS J0205-0949) is an extremely metal poor galaxy, identified in kniazev2003 . pustilnik2007 notes that it exhibits a warped disk at both edges, a feature attributed to tidal interactions, and is rather luminous for such a low metallicity galaxy. NGC 2708-Dw1 is the reddest ( g- = 0.5 ; bennet2018 ), followed by NGC 5631-Dw1 and KDG 013 ( g- = 0.4 ), UGC 9050-Dw1 ( g- = 0.3 ; fielder2023 ), and KUG 0203-Dw1 ( g- = 0.2 ).

gmw_00005635.name: KDG 013
gmw_00005635.overview: KDG 013 is an ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy located in the south-west part of the NGC 830 group [arXiv:2401.01931]. It contains six globular clusters, exhibits faint stellar material extending in a northeast-southwest direction, and is situated near H I gas that is likely unrelated to the galaxy itself [arXiv:2401.01931].

sources.summary: The paper reports KDG 013 as a UDG with typical globular cluster abundance, nearby but likely unrelated H I gas, group membership, diffuse morphology, and tidal-heating interpretation.

quotes.quote: Using Hubble Space Telescope Advanced Camera for Surveys F555W and F814W imaging, we identify 8 2 globular clusters (GCs) in KUG 0203-Dw1 and 6 2 in KDG 013, abundances that are fairly typical for normal dwarf galaxies of similar stellar mass. Jansky Very Large Array data reveal a clear \ detection of KUG 0203-Dw1 with a gas mass estimate of M_ \ /M_ 7.4 and evidence of active stripping by the host. \ gas is found near the location of KDG 013 but is likely unrelated to the UDG itself due to the morphology and the numerous gas tails within the host group.

KDG 013 was first identified in karachentseva1968 , and was also identified in HYPERLEDA paturel2003 and the southern SMUDGes catalog (Systematically Measuring Ultra-diffuse Galaxies; zaritsky2022 ). It lies in the south-west part of the NGC 830 group, with faint stellar material extending predominantly in the northeast southwest directions but not directly connected to any other galaxy within the group (see fig:HI_img right column). For KDG 013 we adopt the distance of NGC 830 which is the approximate average distance of the other group members, ranging from 54.1 3.6 to 58.7 4.1 Mpc. However, despite it's morphology, it is unclear which galaxy in the group the UDG has interacted with.

gmw_00005636.name: UGC 9050-Dw1
gmw_00005636.overview: UGC 9050-Dw1 is a highly amorphous ultra-diffuse galaxy [arXiv:2306.06164, arXiv:2401.01931] that features a blue core with ultraviolet emission [arXiv:2306.06164, arXiv:2401.01931] and an associated stellar tidal plume [arXiv:2306.06164, arXiv:2401.01931]. It contains neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:2306.06164] and an exceptionally large population of globular clusters [arXiv:2306.06164, arXiv:2401.01931].

sources.summary: UGC 9050-Dw1 is a disturbed ultra-diffuse galaxy with UV emission, H I gas, a tidal plume, many globular clusters, and evidence favoring formation through a dwarf merger.

quotes.quote: We investigate the ultra-diffuse galaxy (UDG) UGC 9050-Dw1, which was selected because of its disturbed morphology as part of a larger sample of UDGs that display evidence for significant interactions. We use the Hubble Space Telescope's Advanced Camera for Surveys to identify globular clusters (GCs) associated with UGC 9050-Dw1, and the Jansky Very Large Array to measure its \ content. UGC 9050-Dw1, a neighbor to the low surface brightness spiral UGC 9050, exhibits a unique UV bright central ``clump'' with clearly associated \ gas and an extended stellar tidal plume to the north.

We identify 52 12 GCs, implying a specific frequency S_ N = 122 38 , one of the highest reported for a UDG of this luminosity ( L _ V / L _ = 7.5 0.1 ). Additionally, 20 of the total light of the galaxy is contributed by GCs. Nearly uniform GC colors suggest they were formed during a single intense episode of star formation. We posit that UGC 9050-Dw1 represents the initial definitive observational example of UDG formation resulting from a dwarf merger event, where subsequent clumpy star formation has contributed to its present observed characteristics.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 9050-Dw1 as a large amorphous UDG with a tidal plume, blue core, extraordinary globular cluster population, UV detection, and unusual size-luminosity properties.

quotes.quote: The other three - UGC 9050-Dw1, KUG 0203-Dw1, and KDG 013 - in contrast have GALEX UV detections which is suggestive of recent star formation ( <100 Myr) and also exhibit bluer optical colors. UGC 9050-Dw1 is a highly amorphous UDG with a distinct tidal plume, in the proximity of UGC 9050, but with no clear stellar or \ streams between the two. UGC 9050-Dw1 is larger, with an approximated radius of 6 kpc (though this is not the half-light radius because of the complex morphology of the UDG). NGC 5631-Dw1 has a much fainter surface brightness than the other UDGs, as does UGC 9050-Dw1 outside of the ``core'' region.

UGC 9050-Dw1 is a clear outlier in the size-luminosity relation, with the remaining four UDGs consistent with the Coma cluster and group UDGs. In terms of central surface brightness-half light radius parameter space, UGC 9050-Dw1 is somewhat consistent with the \ UDG population (although still rather large) while NGC 5631-Dw1 is an outlier due to its central surface brightness. NGC 2708-Dw1, NGC 5631-Dw1, and KUG 0203-Dw1 have associated stellar streams connecting to a massive host.

gmw_00005637.name: KUG 0203-100
gmw_00005637.overview: KUG 0203-100 is a luminous, extremely metal-poor galaxy that serves as the primary member of its group [arXiv:2401.01931]. It features a warped disk at both edges and a disturbed morphology, which are attributed to tidal interactions with companions such as KUG 0203-Dw1 [arXiv:2401.01931].

sources.summary: The paper describes KUG 0203-100 as the primary group member: an extremely metal-poor, luminous galaxy with a warped disk and disturbed morphology plausibly affected by KUG 0203-Dw1.

quotes.quote: a http://classic.sdss.org/dr4/algorithms/sdssUBVRITransform.html Both of the UDGs that we present new data for in this work, KUG 0203-Dw1 and KDG 013, have associated galaxies in their respective groups which we briefly describe below. KUG 0203-100: This galaxy is the primary group member, with KUG 0203-Dw1 as the closest companion in addition to 3 other small galaxies. KUG 0203-100 (also known as SDSS J0205-0949) is an extremely metal poor galaxy, identified in kniazev2003 . pustilnik2007 notes that it exhibits a warped disk at both edges, a feature attributed to tidal interactions, and is rather luminous for such a low metallicity galaxy.

gmw_00005638.name: SDSS J012032.19-005501.9
gmw_00005638.overview: SDSS J012032.19-005501.9 is an unobscured type II active galactic nucleus candidate located at a redshift of 0.601 [arXiv:1412.2776]. The galaxy varies significantly on timescales of years, and its faint continuum luminosity suggests the active nucleus may be switching off [arXiv:1412.2776].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J012032.19-005501.9 as a variable, high-redshift unobscured type II AGN candidate lacking broad emission lines and possibly switching off.

quotes.quote: We report the finding of an unobscured type II Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) candidate, SDSS J012032.19-005501.9 at a relatively high redshift of 0.601, which shows a number of unusual properties. It varies significantly on timescales of years as typical type I AGNs and marginally on timescales of weeks. The color-magnitude relation and the structure function are also consistent with that of type I AGNs, which imply that its variability likely originates from the black hole accretion system.

These properties suggest that SDSS J012032.19-005501.9 may be an unobscured quasar without broad emission lines intrinsically, namely an unobscured type II AGN or ``true'' type II AGN. Furthermore, its continuum luminosity is at least one order of magnitude fainter than the average value of the past century expected from the [ OIII ] emission line. It indicates that SDSS J012032.19-005501.9 may be switching off.

gmw_00005639.name: GP101157
gmw_00005639.overview: GP101157 is a Green Pea galaxy characterized by an unresolved morphology in SDSS imaging [arXiv:2110.07741]. It exhibits extreme nebular emission lines, which give the galaxy its distinctive green color [arXiv:2110.07741].

sources.summary: The paper observes GP101157 as one of three Green Pea galaxies, presenting its SDSS identifier, OSIRIS spectrum, extreme emission-line properties, and chemical/stellar-population modelling.

quotes.quote: The sample consists in three galaxies selected among the brightest GPs in the sample presented by . Their SDSS references are: http://skyserver.sdss.org/dr16/en/tools/explore/summary.aspx?plate=458&mjd=51929&fiber=185 SDSSJ030321.41-075923.2 , http://skyserver.sdss.org/dr16/en/tools/explore/summary.aspx?plate=1745&mjd=53061&fiber=463 SDSSJ101157.08+130822.0 and http://skyserver.sdss.org/dr16/en/tools/explore/summary.aspx?plate=1766&mjd=53468&fiber=557 SDSSJ121903.98+152608.5 . The first GP can also be found in the SHOC catalogue (SDSS HII-galaxies with Oxygen abundances Catalog) by kniazev_strong_2004 as SHOC148. For simplicity's sake, however, the GPs will be referred within this manuscript as GP030321, GP101157 and GP121903.

In Fig. fig:sample_spectra , we present the OSIRIS spectra of the three observed galaxies in the rest frame. Both the spectra and the images illustrate the characteristic properties of GP galaxies, namely unresolved morphology in SDSS and green colour due to extreme nebular emission, in particular high EW( [ OIII ] 4959,5007 ) and emission lines falling in the '-band. Moreover, in GP101157 a telluric absorption has a detrimental impact on the HeI6678 line.

gmw_00005640.name: IC 2006
gmw_00005640.overview: IC 2006 is an early-type galaxy in the Fornax cluster classified as either an elliptical or a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1702.02013, arXiv:astro-ph/0502037]. It features a faint, counter-rotating outer H I ring that is visible in ultraviolet light [arXiv:1702.02013, arXiv:astro-ph/0502037].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 2006 as an E1 or lenticular galaxy with a faint outer ring, an HI counter-rotating ring, prominent UV ring emission, and bluer outskirts.

quotes.quote: IC 2006 The galaxy is classified E1 both in the RSA and Buta15 , and lenticular with an outer ring (RLA.-..) in the RC3. The B-band deep surface photometry by Schweizer revealed indeed a faint ring. These authors, however, suggest that the entire profile, including the ring which corresponds to 1-2 of the integrated B-band luminosity, is well approximated by a ^ 1/4 law, i.e. the galaxy is a "bona fide" elliptical. Schweizer found that the ring overlaps with an HI counter-rotating ring at a radius of about 150 , inclined about 37.5 2 with respect to the plane of the sky. In our images the outer ring starts to be recognizable in the U band and becomes more prominent towards W2 (Figure IC2006-sb , top panel).

The presence of the ring reflects on the NUV luminosity profiles as shown in the bottom panel of Figure IC2006-sb . In NIR-optical CGS luminosity profiles this feature is not detected. In this context, we decide to exclude the ring, and to fit the S ersic law of our NUV profiles out to 90 .

sources.summary: The review identifies IC 2006 as an early-type Fornax galaxy with an extended, axisymmetric H I ring and a strongly rising mass-to-light ratio indicating a dark halo.

quotes.quote: One such example is the early-type galaxy IC 2006 in the Fornax cluster. This galaxy has a remarkably axisymmetric HI ring extending from 0.5-6R_e , with circular velocity nearly constant in this range of radii. In this galaxy, M/L rises strongly with radius, signifying a nearly axisymmetric dark halo [ 41 ] .

gmw_00005641.name: G6
gmw_00005641.overview: G6 is a bright K-band galaxy with a redshift of approximately 0.161 [arXiv:2105.07714]. Both its imaging and spectral characteristics indicate that it is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2105.07714].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G6 as one of the bright K-band galaxies near VLSS J2217.5+5943; its spectrum matches an elliptical template and gives a cluster redshift near 0.161.

quotes.quote: We selected the three galaxies G2, G6, and G16, which are among to the brightest in the K band in our sample, with the aim of obtaining spectroscopic redshifts. Fig. fig:spectra shows the smoothed observed spectra of G2 (black), G6 (green), and G16 (red). We used the template of an elliptical galaxy from the SDSS DR5, spectral template 24; Strauss_2002 because both the images (Figs. fig:UGPS_Gray , fig:IRAC_Gray ) and the spectral characteristics indicate that G6 and G16 appear to be of type E. Figure fig:spectra_G6_G16 shows the composite spectra of G6 (green line) and G16 (red line) superimposed on the SDSS template spectrum (grey lines) of an elliptical galaxy. We derived a redshift of z = 0.165 .001 for G16 and a redshift of z = 0.161 .001 for G6 (Table tab:spec_results ).

gmw_00005642.name: G16
gmw_00005642.overview: G16 is a bright K-band galaxy with the spectral characteristics and appearance of an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2105.07714]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy indicate a redshift of z = 0.165 [arXiv:2105.07714].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G16 as a bright K-band galaxy with elliptical spectral characteristics, deriving z = 0.165 and associating it spatially with VLSS J2217.5+5943.

quotes.quote: We selected the three galaxies G2, G6, and G16, which are among to the brightest in the K band in our sample, with the aim of obtaining spectroscopic redshifts. Fig. fig:spectra shows the smoothed observed spectra of G2 (black), G6 (green), and G16 (red). We used the template of an elliptical galaxy from the SDSS DR5, spectral template 24; Strauss_2002 because both the images (Figs. fig:UGPS_Gray , fig:IRAC_Gray ) and the spectral characteristics indicate that G6 and G16 appear to be of type E. Figure fig:spectra_G6_G16 shows the composite spectra of G6 (green line) and G16 (red line) superimposed on the SDSS template spectrum (grey lines) of an elliptical galaxy. We derived a redshift of z = 0.165 .001 for G16 and a redshift of z = 0.161 .001 for G6 (Table tab:spec_results ).

gmw_00005643.name: G2
gmw_00005643.overview: G2 is an inclined disk galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.0422 [arXiv:2105.07714]. It is classified as a LINER, possibly of type 1, and hosts a faint central radio source [arXiv:2105.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes G2 as a foreground, inclined disk galaxy and possible LINER type 1, with central radio emission and redshift z = 0.0422.

quotes.quote: Galaxy G2 shows strong H , [ N ii ] 6548, 6584 , and [ S ii ] 6717, 6731 emission lines -- and in addition the Ca ii IR triplet (8498, 8542, 8662 ) in absorption -- at a redshift of z = 0.0422 .0001 (see Fig. fig:spectra_G2 ). It does not show as strong TiO bands as in the other two cases, the strongest band head is that of the TiO 7053 band. A faint radio source is clearly visible close to the centre of the bright galaxy G2. Our optical spectrum of G2 allowed us to measure the diagnostic line ratios [ N ii ] 6548,6584/H and [ S ii ] 6717,6731/H .

Based on the standard demarcation lines in the BPT diagrams Kewley_2001, Kauffmann_2003 , G2 is a LINER. Moreover, there might be an additional weak broad H component, indicating that it is a LINER type 1 galaxy. G2 is the brightest and largest galaxy in the field. It can be obviously classified as a disk galaxy at a large inclination angle.

gmw_00005644.name: KK211
gmw_00005644.overview: KK211 is a faint dwarf spheroidal satellite galaxy of NGC 5128 [arXiv:0710.1550]. It hosts a central, low-metallicity intermediate-age globular cluster that is interpreted as a nuclear star cluster [arXiv:0710.1550].

sources.summary: The paper studies KK211 as a faint Centaurus A-group dwarf spheroidal whose central intermediate-age globular cluster is interpreted as a nuclear star cluster with low metallicity and a measured mass.

quotes.quote: We present VLT/FORS2 spectroscopic observations of globular clusters (GCs) in five low surface brightness (LSB) dwarf galaxies: KK211 and KK221, which are both dwarf spheroidal satellites (dSph) of NGC 5128, dSph KK84 located close to the isolated S0 galaxy NGC 3115, and two isolated dwarf irregular (dIrr) galaxies UGC 3755 and ESO 490-17.

GCs in KK211 We find another intermediate-age GCs ( 6 2 Gyr) in KK211, which appears to be the central star cluster of this dSph galaxy. The cluster age and metallicity estimates are consistent with the brightest intermediate-age AGB stars in this galaxy, 4 1 Gyr, = -1.4 0.2 , measured by .
We find 2.5 10^5 M_ for KK211-149 and 2 10^6 M_ for KK84-830.

gmw_00005645.name: KK221
gmw_00005645.overview: KK221 is a very faint dwarf spheroidal satellite galaxy of NGC 5128 [arXiv:0710.1550]. It hosts old, metal-poor globular clusters and exhibits a flat surface-brightness profile that suggests the galaxy may be tidally disrupted [arXiv:0710.1550].

sources.summary: The paper treats KK221 as a very faint Centaurus A-group dwarf spheroidal with old metal-poor globular clusters, difficult central association, and possible tidal disruption in its surface-brightness profile.

quotes.quote: We present VLT/FORS2 spectroscopic observations of globular clusters (GCs) in five low surface brightness (LSB) dwarf galaxies: KK211 and KK221, which are both dwarf spheroidal satellites (dSph) of NGC 5128, dSph KK84 located close to the isolated S0 galaxy NGC 3115, and two isolated dwarf irregular (dIrr) galaxies UGC 3755 and ESO 490-17.

GCs in KK221 The two brightest GCs in KK221 are old ( t>10 Gyr) and metal-poor [ Z/H ] 1.5 ). It is difficult to assess whether these objects are associated with the center of K221 due to the very faint surface brightness profile of the host galaxy. However, both seem to be gravitationally bound judging from their radial velocities. A mean radial velocity of all GCs in KK221 coincides well with the radial velocity of the brightest GC. We find a radial velocity anisotropy (rotation) among the GCs at the 95 \ confidence level, in the sense that the radial velocities of GCs 608, 883, and 966 located in the western edge of KK221 differ systematically from the radial velocities of GCs 1090, 24n, and 27n located in the eastern edge of the galaxy.

The Sersic index n appears to be similar for all our sample dwarf galaxies, except KK221, which may be tidally disrupted. The SB profile for KK221 is two times flatter than for the other sample galaxies.

gmw_00005646.name: KK84
gmw_00005646.overview: KK84 is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy located close to the isolated S0 galaxy NGC 3115 [arXiv:0710.1550]. The galaxy contains old globular clusters with ages exceeding 8 Gyr, including a likely central nuclear cluster [arXiv:0710.1550].

sources.summary: The paper describes KK84 as a dwarf spheroidal near NGC 3115, with old globular clusters, a likely nuclear cluster, and some clusters possibly associated with NGC 3115 instead.

quotes.quote: We present VLT/FORS2 spectroscopic observations of globular clusters (GCs) in five low surface brightness (LSB) dwarf galaxies: KK211 and KK221, which are both dwarf spheroidal satellites (dSph) of NGC 5128, dSph KK84 located close to the isolated S0 galaxy NGC 3115, and two isolated dwarf irregular (dIrr) galaxies UGC 3755 and ESO 490-17.

t GCs in KK084 All observed globular clusters in this galaxy are old with ages >8 Gyr. The central GC in KK84, KK084-3-830, has the lowest metallicity and the oldest age among all GCs in this galaxy. We find two metal-poor [ Z/H ] 1.4 ) and two metal-rich clusters [ Z/H ] 0.3 ).
Two clusters appear to be the nuclei of KK84 and KK211. The confirmed globular clusters are in general old and metal-poor, and show a range of \ ratios.

gmw_00005647.name: ESO 490-17
gmw_00005647.overview: ESO 490-17 is a highly isolated dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:0710.1550]. It hosts metal-poor globular clusters that span a wide range of ages, indicating an extended star formation history [arXiv:0710.1550].

sources.summary: The paper treats ESO 490-17 as a highly isolated dwarf irregular whose globular clusters are metal-poor but span ages, supporting an extended star-formation history.

quotes.quote: UGC 3755 Strictly speaking UGC 3755 is a borderline low surface brightness galaxy according to the definition outlined by : _ 0,B 22.5 - 23 mag/arcsec ^ 2 . , which hosts one of the richest GC system among isolated dwarf galaxies (see Table dwgprop ), and ESO490-17 are both highly isolated dIrrs.

GCs in ESO490-17 The two GCs in our sample that are associated with ESO490-17 are both metal-poor. Although our data for this galaxy has the lowest S/N, the age difference between the two GCs with high-quality spectra is seen clearly in the diagnostic plots of Figure diagd . This difference is reflected in the integrated colors (see Table gcprop ). In terms of projected distance, the young cluster GC 2035 is located closer to the galaxy center.

Globular clusters in the two isolated, relatively bright dwarf galaxies UGC 3755 and ESO 490-17 show a wide range of ages from 1 to 9 Gyr, and imply extended star formation histories in these galaxies.

gmw_00005648.name: NGC 4470
gmw_00005648.overview: NGC 4470 is classified as a bulgeless spiral galaxy [arXiv:1612.06794]. X-ray observations of the galaxy have been fitted with an absorbed power law, though no nuclear radio source has been detected at its position [arXiv:1612.06794].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies NGC 4470 as a bulgeless spiral, analyzes its X-ray observations, and reports no detected nuclear radio source.

quotes.quote: NGC 4470 has been observed multiple times by both and . Here we analyze one and two observations that provided a sufficient number of counts for a reliable fit. The observation dates range from 2000 to 2015. In all observations the spectrum was fitted with an absorbed power law.
No radio source was detected at the position of NGC 4470 on a naturally weighted image using the full spectral range. We obtain a 3 upper limit of 21 Jy.
Source specific comments -- IR NGC 4470 : we find that NGC 4470 is a bulgeless spiral galaxy. Therefore, we can not estimate the M_ BH from the BH - bulge scaling relations.

gmw_00005649.name: NGC 2347
gmw_00005649.overview: NGC 2347 is a star-forming galaxy characterized by its rotating disk of gas [arXiv:1804.05853]. Kinematic observations of the galaxy show that its carbon monoxide rotation velocities are measurably higher than its ionized gas and H-alpha rotation velocities [arXiv:1804.05853].

sources.summary: The survey uses NGC 2347 as a worked kinematic example, showing CO and ionized-gas velocity fields and finding its CO rotation velocities exceed H-alpha while ionized lines agree.

quotes.quote: The CO and \ velocity fields for NGC 2347 are shown in Figure fig:COHaVfields . The rotation curves were derived as described in Section ssec:rcfitting .

The majority of our galaxies, however, have CO rotation velocities which are measurably higher than the \ rotation velocities (such as for NGC 2347, shown in Figure fig:RCcomp b). In no case is the \ rotation velocity measurably higher than the CO.

Rotation curves from these lines (as well as CO and ) are shown for NGC 2347 in Figure fig:ionRC , where the colored shading indicates the errors on the rotation curves. Within these errors, the ionized gas rotation curves are consistent with one another, and below the CO rotation curve.

gmw_00005650.name: MCG+04-28-097
gmw_00005650.overview: MCG+04-28-097 is a large cD elliptical galaxy with extremely high ellipticity, located at a redshift of z = 0.1429 [arXiv:2308.01464]. It serves as the brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 1413 [arXiv:2308.01464] and hosts a radio counterpart with a steep power-law spectrum [arXiv:2308.01464].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Abell 1413’s BCG as MCG+04-28-097, a large elliptical cD galaxy whose radio counterpart has a steep power-law spectrum.

quotes.quote: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 1413 Figure fig:A1413_BCG shows a zoom on the central region of Abell 1413, where the BCG and companion head-tail radio galaxy are visible. We overlay radio contours from our high-resolution maps. The BCG itself, catalogued in the literature as MCG+04-28-097 Noonan1972 , is a large cD galaxy with extremely high ellipticity Castagne2012 at redshift z = 0.1429 Humason1956 .

The BCG is detected at high significance in our MeerKAT, JVLA and uGMRT maps in addition to our LOFAR map, where the signal-to-noise ratio is lower due to the reduced sensitivity of LOFAR when adopting uniform weighting. The embedded head-tail radio galaxy is also detected at high significance in our JVLA, MeerKAT, and uGMRT maps; only the presumed core of this head-tail is detected by LOFAR in Figure fig:A1413_BCG . We used these to measure the flux density of the (unresolved) radio counterpart to the BCG. These measurements are listed in Table tab:bcg_fluxes and presented in Figure fig:A1413_BCG_SED ; they indicate no departure from a single power-law behaviour between 145 MHz and 1.519 GHz.
We fitted a single power-law model to the data, from which we derive a spectral index = -1.13^ +0.07 _ 0.06 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies MCG+04-28-097 as the ACO 1413 BCG embedded in the mini-halo, whose source subtraction and blending affect the observed diffuse emission and spectral maps.

quotes.quote: 2019A&A...622A..24S , in their Figure 6, first noted that there are in fact two sources embedded within this mini-halo, and these are clearly visible in this image; S1 being the BCG with optical counterpart MCG+04-28-097 and S2 being the radio source FIRST J115518.6+232422. A localised peak south-west of these sources is also visible, marked with a small blue cross in Figure fig:A1413_radio_mini_halo a, identified as galaxy 2MASS J11551712+2323527 and is blended with the mini-halo. The morphology is similar to that shown in the unsubtracted image of 2019A&A...622A..24S .

These sources blended to give one unresolved source at a 12 resolution and thus the spectral index quoted in Table tab:Table 3 of ^ 1.5 GHz _ 1 GHz = 0.89 0.26 is of this combined source. Sources S1 and S2 obscure the view of the underlying mini-halo spectral distribution, which is evident for all our spectral index maps.

gmw_00005651.name: BBG 9
gmw_00005651.overview: BBG 9 is a candidate Balmer-break galaxy at a high redshift of approximately z~6 [arXiv:1912.10954]. While it is occasionally modeled as a dusty galaxy with an old stellar population [arXiv:1912.10954], its central active galactic nucleus has a bolometric luminosity of 2.7 x 10^41 erg/s [arXiv:1912.10954].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes BBG 9 as a z~6 Balmer-break galaxy candidate, noting rare dusty-galaxy fits while favoring high-redshift stellar-population and progenitor interpretations.

quotes.quote: We removed 17 DGs at z 2 among the 1,000 MC-best models for BBG 9 to calculate the confidence ranges. a All MC-best solutions fell in the same value. We note that none of the 1,000 MC realizations result in the DG/DNLE solutions except for BBG 9. In BBG 9, our MC realizations result in DG solutions on rare occasions (1.7 occurrence rate) The DG solutions for BBG 9 have old stellar populations with T_ age 3 Gyr, a short star-formation time-scale of _ SFH = 0.06 Gyr, and dust attenuation of A_V>3 . We summarize the physical properties of the best-fit BBG models in Table tb:fitprop , where the uncertainties are derived from the MC realizations excluding the DG solutions for BBG 9.

In the MC-best TORUS models, the bolometric luminosity emitted by the central AGN, which is one of the model parameters Fritz+06 , is L_ bol = (2.7^ +1.1 _ 0.7 ) , (3.0^ +14.1 _ 1.4 ) , and (3.3^ +3.2 _ 1.1 ) 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 for BBG 9, 22, and 29, respectively. Finally, SED fitting with the XzLBG group templates results in the MC-best templates at 17 < z < 20 , 19 < z < 26 , and 19 < z < 27 (68 confidence interval from 300 MC runs) for BBG 9, 22, and 29, respectively.

gmw_00005652.name: BBG 22
gmw_00005652.overview: BBG 22 is a high-redshift Balmer-break galaxy candidate that remains undetected in ALMA Band 7 observations [arXiv:1912.10954]. The object has marginal optical-band detections and exhibits a blue Spitzer IRAC color, which favors an extreme Lyman-break galaxy model [arXiv:1912.10954].

sources.summary: The paper presents BBG 22 as an ALMA-undetected Balmer-break candidate, discusses marginal optical-band detections, and notes its blue IRAC color favors an extreme high-redshift LBG model.

quotes.quote: For wavelengths longer than 10 m and X-ray, we used the publicly available catalogs constructed by the individual survey teams ( sec:data ). lcccccc Observed properties of the BBG candidates Name & R.A. & Dec. & K & [ 3.6 ] & [ 4.5 ] & ALMA Band 7 & (degree) & (degree) & (mag) & (mag) & (mag) & (mag) 7 Sample without ALMA detections SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 09 & 149.680749 & 2.062202 & 25.61 0.31 & 23.74 0.19 & 23.54 0.17 & > 19.60 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 22 & 150.071625 & 2.645838 & > 26.2 & 23.99 0.23 & 24.25 0.25 & > 19.59 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 29 & 149.724422 & 1.757402 & > 26.2 & 24.10 0.27 & 23.99 0.26 & > 19.59 7 Sample with ALMA detections SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 19 & 150.074593 & 2.045192 & > 26.2 & 23.94 0.22 & 23.88 0.18 & 15.21 0.03 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 27 & 149.825170 & 2.084366 & > 26.2 & 24.09 0.26 & 24.01 0.26 & 16.46 0.09 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 30 & 150.220922 & 2.607786 & > 26.2 & 24.11 0.26 & 23.96 0.19 & 15.91 0.07 The 2 limiting magnitudes are shown for the fainter objects. Exceptions were found for the i and z bands of BBG 22 as well as the i band of BBG 29, where S/N reached as high as 3 by few combinations of the aperture sizes and sky annuli. Even with the brightest measurements in the i and z bands ( i band) for BBG 22 (BBG 29), our results based on the SED analysis remain unchanged ( sec:SEDfit ). As mentioned in sec:StrictSelection , BBG 22 and BBG 29 are possibly detected with > 2 in the HSC i or z bands. Especially for BBG 22, the observed blue [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] color prefers the XzLBG model rather than the BBG and TORUS models (Figure fig:ALMABBG22_SED ).

gmw_00005653.name: BBG 29
gmw_00005653.overview: BBG 29 is a high-redshift galaxy candidate identified by its Balmer break [arXiv:1912.10954]. It is not detected in ALMA Band 7 observations, but it has possible marginal detections in the optical i band [arXiv:1912.10954].

sources.summary: The paper presents BBG 29 as an ALMA-undetected Balmer-break candidate, corrects an overestimated catalog magnitude, and finds the SED conclusions robust to marginal optical detections.

quotes.quote: For wavelengths longer than 10 m and X-ray, we used the publicly available catalogs constructed by the individual survey teams ( sec:data ). lcccccc Observed properties of the BBG candidates Name & R.A. & Dec. & K & [ 3.6 ] & [ 4.5 ] & ALMA Band 7 & (degree) & (degree) & (mag) & (mag) & (mag) & (mag) 7 Sample without ALMA detections SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 09 & 149.680749 & 2.062202 & 25.61 0.31 & 23.74 0.19 & 23.54 0.17 & > 19.60 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 22 & 150.071625 & 2.645838 & > 26.2 & 23.99 0.23 & 24.25 0.25 & > 19.59 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 29 & 149.724422 & 1.757402 & > 26.2 & 24.10 0.27 & 23.99 0.26 & > 19.59 7 Sample with ALMA detections SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 19 & 150.074593 & 2.045192 & > 26.2 & 23.94 0.22 & 23.88 0.18 & 15.21 0.03 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 27 & 149.825170 & 2.084366 & > 26.2 & 24.09 0.26 & 24.01 0.26 & 16.46 0.09 SPLASH COSMOS z6BBG 30 & 150.220922 & 2.607786 & > 26.2 & 24.11 0.26 & 23.96 0.19 & 15.91 0.07 The 2 limiting magnitudes are shown for the fainter objects. Among the six candidates, only BBG 29 is in the HSC-SSP catalog Aihara+19 . By close inspection, we found that the catalog magnitude of BBG 29 was actually overestimated because of the locally enhanced background sky fluctuation.

Exceptions were found for the i and z bands of BBG 22 as well as the i band of BBG 29, where S/N reached as high as 3 by few combinations of the aperture sizes and sky annuli. Even with the brightest measurements in the i and z bands ( i band) for BBG 22 (BBG 29), our results based on the SED analysis remain unchanged ( sec:SEDfit ). As mentioned in sec:StrictSelection , BBG 22 and BBG 29 are possibly detected with > 2 in the HSC i or z bands.

gmw_00005654.name: LEDA 851827
gmw_00005654.overview: LEDA 851827 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy located within the A2384 cluster [arXiv:1910.12955, arXiv:2009.02724]. The galaxy's northern radio lobe interacts with the cluster's X-ray filament, causing a physical displacement and distortion of the gas [arXiv:1910.12955, arXiv:2009.02724].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 851827 as an FR I radio galaxy in A2384(S), gives its optical position and cluster redshift, and links its NE lobe to the X-ray bridge.

quotes.quote: The FR I radio galaxy located 100 '' north of the BCG (LEDA 67523) of A2384(S) is detected. We classified this galaxy as FR I is based on its morphology. Two lobes extended in the NE-SW direction are associated with the FR I radio galaxy. From the Simbad astronomical database, we found that the counterpart of the FR I radio galaxy is LEDA 851827. The optical position of LEDA 851827 (J2000 RA:21h52m08.1s; Dec:-19d41m27s) matches well its radio counterpart within the 25 '' beam of the 325 MHz GMRT image.

The spectroscopic redshift of this galaxy is 0.09447; hence, this galaxy is a member of the A2384 cluster. This further supports the observational evidence of the interaction between the NE radio lobe of LEDA 851827 and the X-ray bridge.
The X-ray and radio images show that the NE lobe is displacing the X-ray gas in the east direction and is causing the discontinuity in the X-ray emissivity.

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 851827 as an A2384 member FR I radio galaxy whose northern lobe distorts the cluster X-ray filament and may supply seed particles.

quotes.quote: In , we reported a peculiar detection of the FR I radio galaxy LEDA 851827 (J2000 RA:21h52m08.1s; Dec:-19 ^ 41 ' 27 '' ) associated with A2384(S). Its northern radio lobe distorts the X-ray filament in the easterly direction. In the MeerKAT data, we also noticed the northern radio lobe of 208 270 kpc ^ 2 (119 '' 154 '' ), but that southern radio lobe was missing (Fig.  MeerKAT_radio_img (b)).
In the case of A2384, we detected the diffuse radio sources (relic and candidate radio ridge) in the vicinity of the FR I radio galaxy which could be a natural source of the seed particles.

gmw_00005655.name: SPT0311-58
gmw_00005655.overview: SPT0311-58 is a massive, dusty star-forming system at a redshift of 6.900 [arXiv:1705.07912, arXiv:2203.14972]. It consists of an interacting pair of ultra-luminous and hyper-luminous infrared galaxies [arXiv:2301.02313, arXiv:1811.10644] that exhibit highly clumpy, turbulent structures and rapid star formation [arXiv:2203.14972].

sources.summary: The paper presents SPT0311-58 as a z=6.9 dusty starburst pair with extreme infrared luminosity, high gas mass, rapid clumpy star formation, and perturbed kinematics likely tied to interactions.

quotes.quote: SPT0311-58 is the highest redshift ( z =6.9) infrared-luminous dusty star-forming system known so far Strandet+17,Marrone+18 and one of the only two identified in the Epoch of Reionization (the other is HFLS3 at a redshift of 6.3; Riechers+13 ). This system was first discovered in the South Pole Telescope Survey Vieira+13 and was later studied in detail with ALMA, tracing several molecular and atomic lines at different angular resolutions from 0.3 0.5 to 0.07 0.08 arcsec Marrone+18,Jarugula+21,Spilker+22 . The system consists of an East-lensed (E) and a West-lensed (W) galaxy that are separated by a projected distance of 8 kpc, relative velocities of 700 km s ^ 1 and average lensing magnification of 1.3 (E) and 2.2 (W). Their intrinsic infrared luminosity (8 to 1000 m) places the two galaxies of the system in the range of ultra-luminous (E) and hyper-luminous (W) infrared galaxies, with luminosities of 3.5 0.7 10 ^ 12 L _ and 26 12 10 ^ 12 L _ , respectively Jarugula+21 .

SPT0311-58 is a very gas-rich system with intrinsic total gas masses (E and W) of 3.1 2.7 10 ^ 10 M _ and 5.4 3.4 10 ^ 11 M _ Jarugula+21 . Star formation is proceeding at a very rapid pace, with [ C II ] 158 m-based rates of 778 and 1775 M _ yr ^ 1 in the E and W galaxies, respectively Spilker+22 . This star formation appears to be distributed across several clumps with sizes of about 1 kpc radius, and the stars form at rates of 100 to 400 M _ yr ^ 1 . The kinematics of the [ C II ] emitting gas indicate that neither of the two galaxies in SPT0311-58 agree with the velocity pattern from a thin rotation-supported disc.

sources.summary: The paper reports SPT-S J031132 5823.4 as a z=6.900 DSFG discovered by SPT, with ALMA-confirmed molecular lines, radiative-transfer ISM constraints, and extreme gas mass.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery and constrain the physical conditions of the interstellar medium of the highest-redshift millimeter-selected dusty star-forming galaxy (DSFG) to date, SPT-S J031132 5823.4 (hereafter SPT0311 58), at z=6.900 0.002 . SPT0311 58 was discovered via its 1.4 mm thermal dust continuum emission in the South Pole Telescope (SPT)-SZ survey. The spectroscopic redshift was determined through an ALMA 3 mm frequency scan that detected CO(6--5), CO(7--6) and (2--1), and subsequently confirmed by detections of CO(3--2) with ATCA and \ with APEX. We constrain the properties of the ISM in SPT0311 58 with a radiative transfer analysis of the dust continuum photometry and the CO and \ line emission.
