sources.summary: The paper finds RGB J1629+4008 has HBL-like X-ray properties, rapid X-ray variability, and synchrotron radiation extending into the X-ray band.

quotes.quote: The band in one of our sources, RGB J1629+4008, is dominated by synchrotron emission peaking at 2 10^ 16 Hz, as also shown by its steep (energy index _ x 1.5 ) spectrum. This makes this object the first known FSRQ whose X--ray emission is not due to inverse Compton radiation.

We looked for time variability in every observation, binning the data in intervals from 500 to 4000 s, with null results except for RGB J1629 + 4008. This source, in fact, clearly varied during the observation ( test probability of constancy <10^ 7 , see Fig. fig2 ), doubling its MECS flux in about 15 hours, with a more rapid decay of a factor of 4 in about 7 hours at the end of the observation.

sources.summary: The source is classified as a broad-line radio galaxy, has BeppoSAX X-ray observations, and is unresolved in both the new radio images and FIRST.

quotes.quote: RGB J1629 + 4008. An optical spectrum of this source was published in LM98 who classified it as a broad-line radio galaxy based on an absolute optical magnitude of M>-22 mag. X-ray observations of this source were obtained with Beppo SAX by P02 . The source remains unresolved in the radio on the scales imaged (Fig. 1(e)). Similarly, the VLA B array map from the FIRST survey at 20 cm shows only an unresolved core.

sources.summary: The paper reports JVN 8.4 GHz VLBI detection of RXS J16290+4007 as an unresolved radio-loud NLS1 with high brightness temperature, attributing its radio emission mainly to AGN activity.

quotes.quote: We performed phase-reference very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) observations on five radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies (NLS1s) at 8.4 GHz with the Japanese VLBI Network (JVN). Each of the five targets (RXS J08066+7248, RXS J16290+4007, RXS J16333+4718, RXS J16446+2619, and B3 1702+457) in milli-Jansky levels were detected and unresolved in milli-arcsecond resolutions, i.e., with brightness temperatures higher than 10^7 K. The nonthermal processes of active galactic nuclei (AGN) activity, rather than starbursts, are predominantly responsible for the radio emissions from these NLS1s.

gmw_00005743.name: WGA J0546.6 6415
gmw_00005743.overview: WGA J0546.6 6415 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0208501]. The object exhibits intermediate BL Lac-like behavior and possesses a flat X-ray spectrum with a possible soft X-ray synchrotron tail [arXiv:astro-ph/0208501].

sources.summary: The paper describes WGA J0546.6 6415 as a quasar with flat X-ray spectrum, possible soft X-ray synchrotron tail, and intermediate BL Lac-like behavior.

quotes.quote: The flatness of the X--ray spectrum, as for WGA J0546.6+6415, does not confirm the ``HBL--like" nature of this objects as suggested by the broad band indices. However, the possible steeper component at soft X--ray energies, if confirmed, may be attributed to the tail of the synchrotron emission, thus locating the observed X--ray band in the ``valley" between synchrotron and Compton emissions (see, in fact, Fig. fig7 ). In this case, the quasar could be the FSRQ counterpart of ``intermediate" BL Lacs.

gmw_00005744.name: RGB J1722+2436
gmw_00005744.overview: RGB J1722+2436 is a blazar that may resemble an intermediate BL Lacertae object [arXiv:astro-ph/0208501]. It is characterized as a weak soft X-ray source and is not detected by the Phoswich Detector System [arXiv:astro-ph/0208501].

sources.summary: The paper finds RGB J1722+2436 is the weakest soft-X-ray source in the observed set, is not detected by PDS, and may resemble intermediate BL Lacs.

quotes.quote: RGB J1722+2436 tbh 2truecm 3.3truecm This source is the weakest of the four observed in the soft X--ray range (see the LECS count rates in Tab. tab2 ). To enhance the S/N, therefore, we used a 6 extraction region for the LECS, as suggested by the SDC fio99 . The source is not detected by the PDS.

For WGA J0546.6 6415 and RGB J1722+2436 the situation is less clear. Our fits and the SEDs are not inconsistent with the fact that the band might be close to the ``valley'' between synchrotron and inverse Compton emission, which would classify them as akin to ``intermediate'' BL Lacs.

gmw_00005745.name: ESO 111 22
gmw_00005745.overview: ESO 111 22 is a double-ringed, nonbarred galaxy classified as type (R)SA()b [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features a smooth inner ring and a large, patchy outer ring, both of which contain discrete HII regions [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A double-ringed nonbarred galaxy whose smooth inner ring and patchy outer ring both contain HII regions; the rings are unusually large and asymmetric.

quotes.quote: ESO 111 22 (Figure img1 , top row). A strong example of a double-ringed nonbarred galaxy, type (R)SA()b, with a prominent, smooth inner ring and a large, patchy outer ring seen in the B band. In spite of the obvious B band morphological differences, the two rings of ESO 111 22 both show discrete HII regions, the inner ring being especially prominent. Near-side extinction may be causing the inner ring to be weak on the east side. The rings appear asymmetric; the outer ring, in particular, appears distorted from a smooth oval shape.

gmw_00005746.name: ESO 198 13
gmw_00005746.overview: ESO 198 13 is a nonbarred ringed galaxy classified as type (R)SA()ab [arXiv:1004.5063]. The galaxy features three distinct rings, including a large nuclear ring and an inner ring where its ionized gas is primarily concentrated [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A nonbarred ringed galaxy with nuclear, inner, and outer ring features; its ionized gas is confined mainly to the nuclear and inner rings.

quotes.quote: ESO 198 13 (Figure img1 , middle row). This galaxy is classified as type (R)SA()ab. ESO 198 13 actually has three ring features, but only the inner two are displayed in the image. The small, bright ring close to the nucleus was interpreted by Buta Crocker (1993) and Buta (1995) as a nuclear ring since it is very much the analog of a nuclear ring in a barred galaxy. However, this feature has a linear diameter of 4.76 kpc, considerably larger than the average nuclear ring diameter of 1.5 kpc ( H_o = 75 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 ; Buta Crocker 1993).

gmw_00005747.name: ESO 231 1
gmw_00005747.overview: ESO 231 1 is a nonbarred spiral galaxy classified as an (R ^ )SA()b system [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features an inner ring, which may be doubled, and an outer pseudoring that both show strong H-alpha emission [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A nonbarred galaxy with inner and outer pseudorings strongly represented in H-alpha, with ordered emission following the stellar spiral structure.

quotes.quote: ESO 231 1 (Figure img1 , bottom row). This galaxy is classified as an (R ^ )SA()b. There are two rings, an inner ring and an outer pseudoring. Both features are strongly represented in the narrowband H image. The inner ring appears to be doubled or has a double spiral feature tightly wound into the appearance of a ring.

gmw_00005748.name: ESO 234 11
gmw_00005748.overview: ESO 234 11 is an early-type ringed galaxy classified as an (R)SA()0^+ [arXiv:1004.5063]. While a faint hexagonal outer ring is visible in blue light, the ring structure lacks strong H-alpha emission, indicating it is composed of an older stellar population [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: An early-type ringed galaxy whose faint hexagonal outer ring is visible in blue light but lacks strong H-alpha emission, implying older stellar rings.

quotes.quote: ESO 234 11 (Figure img2 , top row). This galaxy is classified as an (R)SA()0 ^ + . Since ESO 234 11 is an early-type galaxy and does not have a lot of gas present, we would not expect to see strong H emission from it. While the B band image shows a faint hexagonal outer ring, the narrowband image shows very little ionized gas mainly from the center of the galaxy. The rings are not visible in the H image, implying that they are made up of an older stellar population.

gmw_00005749.name: ESO 236 29
gmw_00005749.overview: ESO 236 29 is a relatively normal ringed galaxy classified as (R)SA()a [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features three distinct ring structures, including a nuclear ring and an inner ring composed of discrete HII regions [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A relatively normal ringed galaxy with nuclear, inner, and outer rings; its large apparent ring sizes led to a collisional-ring interpretation.

quotes.quote: ESO 236 29 (Figure img2 , middle row). ESO 236 29 appears to be a relatively normal (R)SA()a galaxy with three ring features. Two of the three ring features are partly seen in the H image: the nuclear and the inner rings. The inner ring appears mostly complete and consists of discrete HII regions. The strongest feature in H is from the center of the galaxy where ionized gas follows a more elongated distribution than the apparent stellar bulge isophotes.

gmw_00005750.name: ESO 286 10
gmw_00005750.overview: ESO 286 10 is an early-type ringed galaxy classified as an (R')SA(r)a [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features a faint, off-center outer pseudoring made primarily of a single spiral arm, and it exhibits very little H-alpha emission throughout the ring and the galaxy as a whole [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: An early-type ringed galaxy with a faint off-center outer pseudoring and only weak H-alpha emission in the ring and galaxy as a whole.

quotes.quote: ESO 286 10 (Figure img2 , bottom row). This galaxy is classified as an (R ^ )SA()a. In the B band image, we see a single, faint, off-center ring which is the outer pseudoring. It appears to be made mostly of a single spiral arm, although a second outer arm is definitely present on the east side. There is very little H emission in the ring-arm or in the galaxy as a whole.

gmw_00005751.name: ESO 297 27
gmw_00005751.overview: ESO 297 27 is classified as an SA(rs)bc spiral galaxy [arXiv:1004.5063]. While its blue light images reveal a single inner arm winding counter to faint outer arms, its patchy and disorganized H-alpha emission shows no trace of this counter-winding structure [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A spiral galaxy with counter-winding structure in blue light but patchy, disorganized H-alpha emission not organized into a ring.

quotes.quote: ESO 297 27 (Figure img3 , top row). This galaxy is classified as an SA(rs)bc and was studied in greater detail in Paper 1. It appears, at first glance, to be a normal intermediate to late-type spiral galaxy. However, a closer inspection of the B band images shows a single inner arm winding counter to faint outer arms. There is no trace of the counter-winding structure in the H emission of the galaxy which appears patchy and disorganized.

gmw_00005752.name: ESO 399 25
gmw_00005752.overview: ESO 399 25 is an early-type galaxy classified as an (R')SA(s)0/a [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features a faint outer stellar ring approximately 32 kpc in diameter that contains little to no ionized gas [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: An early-type galaxy with a faint outer stellar ring but little ionized gas, leaving the broadband and narrowband profiles mostly featureless.

quotes.quote: ESO 399 25 (Figure img3 , middle row). This galaxy is classified as an (R ^ )SA(s)0/a. The B band image shows a faint outer ring, which is not seen in the H image. As an early-type galaxy, there is little or no ionized gas seen in the narrowband image. Using the broadband ring derived parameters, we estimate the outer ring to be 32 kpc in diameter; however, since it has no measurable ionized gas, it is an older stellar component.

gmw_00005753.name: ESO 409 3
gmw_00005753.overview: ESO 409 3 is classified as an (R')SA(s)ab galaxy [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features a faint pseudoring of H-alpha emission that matches its broadband pseudoring, though most of its ionized gas is concentrated near the center [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A ringed galaxy whose faint H-alpha pseudoring follows the broadband pseudoring, while most ionized gas is concentrated near the center.

quotes.quote: ESO 409 3 (Figure img3 , bottom row). This galaxy is classified as an (R ^ )SA(s)ab. The galaxy has a faint pseudoring of H emission, which follows the pseudoring seen in the B band image. Most of the ionized gas is concentrated near the center of the galaxy. We estimate that the pseudoring has a diameter of 36.5 kpc and emits 53 of the galaxy's H .

gmw_00005754.name: ESO 526 7
gmw_00005754.overview: ESO 526 7 is a nonbarred spiral galaxy classified as SA()b [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features a broad inner ring with a diameter of 21.6 kpc, which is traced by patchy H-alpha emission and surrounded by a multi-armed spiral structure [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A nonbarred galaxy with a broad inner ring and multi-armed spiral structure, both traced by patchy H-alpha emission.

quotes.quote: ESO 526 7 (Figure img4 , top row). This galaxy is classified as an SA()b. In the B band image, we note the presence of a broad inner ring with patchy, multi-armed spiral structure continuing beyond the ring. This inner ring is also seen as a patchy narrow ring of emission in the H image. The inner ring has a diameter of 21.6 kpc and emits 15 of the total H flux from the galaxy.

gmw_00005755.name: ESO 576 57
gmw_00005755.overview: ESO 576 57 is classified as an early-type ringed galaxy [arXiv:1004.5063]. It features a thin, partially present outer ring and a more diffuse inner ring, though neither structure shows strong H-alpha emission [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: An early-type ringed galaxy with weak H-alpha emission, smooth profiles, and evidence for diffuse ionized gas despite little ring emission.

quotes.quote: ESO 576 57 (Figure img4 , middle row). This galaxy is classified as an (R)SA()ab. Two rings are seen in the B band image: a thin outer ring and a more diffuse inner ring. Neither feature is exceptionally strong, and the outer ring is only partially present. The H emission does not reflect this structure however.

gmw_00005756.name: IC 5267
gmw_00005756.overview: IC 5267 is a face-on lenticular or early-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1004.5063, arXiv:1901.07562] featuring a bright nucleus and two tightly wound spiral arms that form an outer pseudo-ring [arXiv:2304.00515]. The galaxy contains a broken ring of neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:2304.00515] and a minor axis dust lane that suggests a recent minor merger [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A ringed galaxy with two thin spiral rings traced intermittently by HII regions and a dust lane suggesting a recent minor merger.

quotes.quote: IC 5267 (Figure img5 , top row). This galaxy is classified as an (R)SA(rs)0/a. The broadband image shows two thin rings of spiral structure, which are intermittently traced by HII regions in the narrowband image. Overall, the galaxy has very little H emission; however, the emission it does have appears to follow the spiral arms. The B band image shows a minor axis dust lane, suggesting that IC 5267 has suffered a recent minor merger that may account for its complex structure.

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 5267 as the face-on host galaxy of SN 2011hs and argues that its dust likely dominates the observed interstellar polarization and may differ from Milky Way dust.

quotes.quote: It is located at R.A. = 22 ^ h 57 ^ m 11 ^ s and = -43 23'04" in the galaxy IC 5267 (face-on sA0) with redshift z = 0.005711, corresponding to a recessional velocity of 1714 koribalski04 . Host galaxy of SN 2011hs and Serkowski law From the upper limits on the Galactic ISP derived in Section sec:gal_isp we can conclude that most of the ISP in the data of SN 2011hs originated from IC 5267. In order to do so, a value for the reddening of IC 5267 must be calculated. Therefore, the assumption that the dust of IC 5267 or in the vicinity of SN 2011hs is similar to that of the Milky Way may not be correct. This could suggest that the dust in IC 5267 or in the local neighbourhood of SN 2011hs maybe different from that in the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5267 as an SA0/a ring galaxy with a bright nucleus, tightly wound arms forming an outer pseudo-ring, broken HI ring, and ordered rotation.

quotes.quote: IC 5267: This galaxy is classified a SA0/a(s) and is observed to have a bright nucleus and two low surface brightness spiral arms tightly wound to form an outer pseudo-ring. A broken ring of 1 gas is observed to roughly follow the outer pseudo-ring. The velocity map of this galaxy reflects fairly ordered rotational motion. See the top right panels in Fig. fig:mosaic4 for the optical image and the 1 velocity map of this galaxy.

gmw_00005757.name: NGC 5530
gmw_00005757.overview: NGC 5530 is classified as an SA(s) galaxy with a patchy and disorganized spiral structure [arXiv:1004.5063]. While its blue light emissions suggest the presence of an inner pseudoring, its unorganized and randomly distributed HII regions do not outline this structure [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: A patchy spiral galaxy with an inner pseudoring in blue light, unorganized HII regions, and H-alpha enhancements tied to ring and spiral structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 5530 (Figure img5 , bottom row). This galaxy is classified as an SA( s). The spiral structure is patchy and disorganized in both the B band and H images. We do get the sense of an inner pseudoring in the B band, which is not seen outlined by the HII regions. These HII regions, instead, appear unorganized and randomly distributed.

gmw_00005758.name: NGC 7187
gmw_00005758.overview: NGC 7187 is an early-type galaxy classified with a morphology that includes a very faint outer ring and a striking, symmetric inner ring [arXiv:1004.5063]. Although the galaxy lacks a strong bar, its bulge exhibits isophote twists and may contain up to three bar-like features [arXiv:1004.5063].

sources.summary: An early-type galaxy with a faint outer ring and symmetric inner ring; only the inner ring is strong in H-alpha, suggesting recent star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 7187 (Figure img6 , middle row). This galaxy is classified as (R)S A B()0 ^+ . The morphology consists of a very faint outer ring and a striking inner ring, which appears normal and symmetric. There appears to be no strong bar, but the bulge shows isophote twists, and there may be up to three bar-like features (Buta 1990a; Wozniak et al. 1995).

gmw_00005759.name: galaxy 375
gmw_00005759.overview: Galaxy 375 is an early-type galaxy characterized by extended far-ultraviolet emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0301364]. This emission is contaminated by Lyman-alpha emission, which makes the galaxy's ultraviolet-upturn color measurement an upper limit [arXiv:astro-ph/0301364].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy 375 as the sample object with extended far-UV emission, contaminated by Lyman emission, making its UV-upturn color only an upper limit.

quotes.quote: Except for galaxy 375 (which has extended emission), there is little variation in our m_ 1550 V colors as we reduce the aperture size -- they remain uniformly red, with small variations consistent with the statistical uncertainties in Table 1.

Except for galaxy 375, all of the galaxies show weak UV upturn emission. Galaxy 375 is contaminated by the Lyman- emission discussed previously, so the UV upturn emission is really an upper limit on the emission from the evolved HB population. Note that in a much smaller aperture (radius 5 pixels) that excludes much of the Lyman- emission, this galaxy has an even bluer m_ 1550 V of 1.6 mag.

gmw_00005760.name: image 11.1
gmw_00005760.overview: The object image 11.1 is a high-redshift galaxy located in the Abell S1063 cluster field [arXiv:1405.0222]. It is a gravitationally lensed, Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy with a spectroscopically confirmed redshift of z=3.117 [arXiv:1405.0222].

sources.summary: The paper identifies image 11.1 in Abell S1063 as a Lyman-emitting lensed galaxy at z=3.117 and reports this as the first spectroscopic redshift for its system.

quotes.quote: Image 11.1 : We identify image 11.1 as an image of a Lyman emitting galaxy at z=3.117 . This spectroscopic redshift is the first measurement for this galaxy and for this multiply imaged system. The Ly 1215 \ line redshifted to 5006 \ in the spectrum of image 11.1, as shown in Figure fig:as1063_spec11_1 in the Appendix, is the only emission line we can confirm in the wavelength range 4000-9800 . We note that this result is consistent with =3.08 0.1 (95 confidence level) measured from CLASH imaging Jouvel:2014qy . We base our redshift measurements on the Ly line only.

gmw_00005761.name: Image 2.1
gmw_00005761.overview: Image 2.1 is a galaxy located in the Abell 2744 cluster field [arXiv:1405.0222]. It has a tentative, low-confidence redshift of z=2.2 based on absorption and possible emission features, which is consistent with its photometric-redshift constraints [arXiv:1405.0222].

sources.summary: The paper reports a tentative z=2.2 redshift for Image 2.1 in Abell 2744 from absorption and possible emission features, noting consistency with photometric-redshift constraints.

quotes.quote: Image 2.1 : We report a possible low-confidence solution of z=2.2 based on the strong absorption features at 4890 and 4950 \ corresponding to Si II 1527 \ and C IV 1549 , and a possible low signal-to-noise emission line at 6110 \ matching C III ] 1909 . This solution is consistent with the BPZ range ( 1.1< <2.4 ); however, the Bayesian probability distribution for the photometric redshift is bimodal, with peaks around 1.2,2.2 . The one-dimensional and two-dimensional spectra of this galaxy are shown in Figure fig:a2744_spec2 in the Appendix. Spectral templates of Lyman break galaxies at z=3 from Shapley:2003fk are plotted with the spectra to guide the eye to common spectral features in these galaxies.

gmw_00005762.name: Image 3.1/2
gmw_00005762.overview: Image 3.1/2 is a strongly lensed source galaxy system located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=3.98 [arXiv:1405.0222]. It is observed as a long, knotty arc that exhibits mirror symmetry across a tangential critical curve [arXiv:1406.2702].

sources.summary: System 3 is a long, knotty arc with mirror-symmetric close images straddling the critical curve; small model shifts change where additional images are predicted.

quotes.quote: System 3. This long arc, which has six knots, exhibits mirror symmetry about its centre, indicating that it comprises a pair of close images , labelled 3.1 and 3.2, straddling the tangential critical curve. The tangential critical curve of this ``caterpillar'' shaped object runs between images 3.1 and 3.2 showing reflection symmetry. Our non-parametric algorithm, however, tends to produce a lens model with the critical curve lying 1'' to the west of 3.1 rather than passing between 3.1 and 3.2; i.e., a difference in the expected position of the critical curve of 2''. As a result, additional images that are obviously not observed are predicted on the other side of the critical curve when we delens and relens 3.1 and 3.2, as shown in figure system3 .

sources.summary: The paper measures z=3.98 for the merging lensed image pair Image 3.1/2 in Abell 2744 and identifies it as the first spectroscopic redshift for that source galaxy system.

quotes.quote: Image 3.1/2 : We measure z=3.98 for the merging pair of images 3.1/2 based on the detection of several absorption line features (Si II 1260 , O I + Si II 1302,1304 , C II* 1334 , and the Si IV doublet 1393,1402 ) and the Ly break around 6100 \ (see Figure fig:a2744_spec3 in the Appendix). The spectroscopic redshift is consistent with the photometric redshift obtained from BPZ, =4.02^ +0.4 _ 0.1 (95 confidence range) for image 3.1 and =3.96^ +0.4 _ 0.2 for image 3.2. This is the first spectroscopic redshift measurement for this system of lensed images mapping to the same source galaxy.

gmw_00005763.name: Image 4.5
gmw_00005763.overview: Image 4.5 is a lensed galaxy image located in the Abell 2744 cluster field [arXiv:1405.0222]. It has a spectroscopic redshift of z=3.58, which is confirmed by matching spectral features with its counter image [arXiv:1405.0222].

sources.summary: The paper discusses Image 4.5 in Abell 2744 as a possible z=3.58 lensed galaxy image, with spectral features matching a counter image of the same background source.

quotes.quote: Image 4.5 : We detect an emission line around 5575 , which may correspond to Ly and a redshift of z=3.58 ; however, this emission line is close to the sky line at 5577 , and with the resolution of our spectrum, it is difficult to distinguish the line from the residuals of the sky subtraction (see Figure fig:a2744_spec4 in the Appendix). We also find a feature around 7100 \ possibly corresponding to C IV 1550 . Richard:2014gf measure a spectroscopic redshift of z=3.58 for the counter image of this galaxy, image 4.3. The agreement between the spectroscopic redshifts of these two objects confirms their identification as multiple images of the same background source.

gmw_00005764.name: Image 10.3
gmw_00005764.overview: Image 10.3 is a high-redshift galaxy located in the field of the Abell 2744 cluster [arXiv:1405.0222]. It has a likely redshift of z=2.735, supported by absorption features such as Si II and C IV, along with possible Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:1405.0222].

sources.summary: The paper finds a likely z=2.735 solution for Image 10.3 in Abell 2744 using absorption features, possible Ly emission, and consistency with the photometric-redshift distribution.

quotes.quote: Image 10.3 : We find a likely redshift solution of z=2.735 for image 10.3 based on the two absorption features around 5600 and 5700 , which we identify as Si II 1527 \ and C IV 1550 \ (see Figure fig:a2744_spec10 in the Appendix). This solution matches with the possibility of Ly emission at 4542 \ and several likely absorption lines (O I + Si II 1302,1304 , C II* 1334 , the Si IV doublet 1393,1402 ). The photometric redshift distribution for this galaxy covers this redshift value, =3.08^ +0.10 _ 0.46 .

gmw_00005765.name: RGB J0152+017
gmw_00005765.overview: RGB J0152+017 is a high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1108.0607] classified as an extreme TeV blazar [arXiv:1210.2802]. Although multi-wavelength light curves show no variability [arXiv:1108.0607, arXiv:2210.09797], long-term optical monitoring has revealed minor flux changes and clear color-magnitude behavior [arXiv:1409.3022].

sources.summary: The paper treats RGB J0152+017 as one of seven extreme TeV blazars and finds it gives the weakest IGMF constraints among the tested sources.

quotes.quote: RGB J0152+017 was observed over the period of October 30 - November 14 2007 for a total exposure of 14.7 hours. A time averaged dFED was reported for six bins over the energy range of 240 GeV - 3.6 TeV ( ). The flux in all but the first energy bin of the Fermi data is strongly detected (TS > 25) for the majority of simulated models.

Figure rgbj0152_B0 a and b illustrate that this source perhaps provides the weakest constraints on the IGMF with nearly the entire parameter space of simulated models compatible with the VHE and HE spectral data. In only some of these models, the HE part of the spectrum is dominated by the primary emission.

sources.summary: The paper fits RGB J0152+017 with primary-electron leptonic emission and a subdominant pp-decay TeV component that improves the spectrum and slightly exceeds WCDA sensitivity.

quotes.quote: RGB J0152+017 The VHE emission of RGB J0152+017 is discovered by HESS at the end of 2007 2008AA...481L.103A . Ref.  2008AA...481L.103A provides the only simultaneous multi-wavelength SED that can be found in literature, and multi-wavelength light curves do not show any significant variability. Our fitting result is given in Figure  RGB0152 .

The SED is well fitted by leptonic emission from primary electrons, and emission from ^0 decay has a sub-dominant contribution at TeV band, improving the fitting of the TeV spectrum to a certain extent. Because of the significant EBL absorption, the flux of model predicted TeV emission decreases rapidly, nevertheless still slightly exceeds the one-year sensitivity of WCDA of LHAASO.

sources.summary: The paper treats RGB J0152+017 as an HBL detected by HESS, with VHE power-law spectrum, simultaneous Swift/RXTE data, Fermi/LAT upper limit, and no SED variability timescale.

quotes.quote: RGB J0152+017. A HBL object was detected by HESS in late October and November 2007 (Aharonian et al. 2008). The observed spectrum is well fit by a power law with 2.95 , and the integral flux above 300 GeV corresponds to 2 of Crab flux. The data of the broadband SED also include the simultaneous observations of Swift and RXTE (Aharonian et al. 2008), and the upper limit detected by Fermi /LAT (from Tavecchio et al. 2010b). No corresponding variability for the SED is found, so we take t=24 h.

sources.summary: Long-term ATOM monitoring found clear color-magnitude behavior in RGB J0152+017, including shorter-period trends, modest flux changes, and optical spectral indices between about 2 and 3.

quotes.quote: RGB J0152+017: The source was observed during 269 nights in 2007--2012. Clear behavior is present in the entire dataset, as well as in shorter isolated periods (with the exception of the earliest measurements at the lowest flux level, when a weak trend could be noted). The observed flux changes were rather minor ( 0.4 mag), and the optical spectrum varied between _ BR 2 and 3.

gmw_00005766.name: NGC 3729
gmw_00005766.overview: NGC 3729 is a barred, late-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:0902.2492, arXiv:1909.13147] classified as SBa (pec) [arXiv:2005.08985]. It is gas-poor compared to normal Sab galaxies [arXiv:0902.2492] and shows evidence of a late-stage minor merger, featuring star-forming nuclear and bar-end rings [arXiv:2005.08985].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3729 as NGC 3718's smaller Sab companion, measuring low H I mass, gas poverty, rotation to 5 kpc, and K-band ellipticity.

quotes.quote: We adopt the distance of 17 \ given by Tu98 for the Ursa Major group: there, 1 \ is equivalent to 4.945 , 1 \ = 82.4 , and 13 \ = 1.07 . NGC 3718 is a very luminous galaxy with L_B 3 10^ 10 , while for NGC 3729 L_B 7 10^ 9 . Tu96 classified NGC 3718 as T=1 (Sa) and NGC 3729 as T=2 (Sab) on the basis of their optical and near-infrared images.
NGC 3729 has only 3 10^8 \ of \ and is gas-poor compared to a normal Sab galaxy; we find =0.04 while =0.1 would be typical rh94 .

The small galaxy NGC 3729 is projected 11 \ to the east, and shows a clear signature of rotation in gas that extends to 1 \ or 5 \ radius. >From their K-band images, find an isophotal ellipticity e = 1-b/a = 0.32 . If we assume the disk to be round (although the galaxy is classified as barred), it is inclined 48 \ from face-on. Further assuming that the \ gas shares this plane yields a dynamical mass M _ dyn = 35 10^9 \ and M/L _K 2 in solar units.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3729 as a peculiar barred SBa galaxy linked to a late minor merger, with star-forming rings, diffuse SFD emission, and gas-rich clumpy absorption.

quotes.quote: In this Appendix, we describe a somewhat unusual barred galaxy, NGC 3729. This has a classification of SBa (pec), with the peculiar nature being linked to a probable late-stage minor merger, as illustrated in the SDSS colour composite image in Fig.  fig:ngc3729_sdss . The low-surface-brightness feature to the north-east of NGC 3729 may be the tidally-shredded remains of a low-mass companion, and the mottled appearance of the upper half of NGC 3729 may be due to dusty gas accreted during this interaction. We adopt a distance of 20.2 Mpc for this system.

Spectroscopically, NGC 3729 shows strong and complex central emission, and emission from a bar-end ring, all of which has [ N ii ] /H ratios fully consistent with star formation; see the 2-dimensional spectrum in Fig.  fig:ngc3729spec , taken with the INT and IDS at a slit position angle of 290 ^ , perpendicular to the bar. The bar-end ring results in the strong peaks of emission towards the top and bottom of this figure, at distance of 19 ^ or 1.8 kpc from the nucleus. The spectrum shows complex nuclear structure, with multiple components, including a very rapidly rotating structure, which we interpret as a star-forming nuclear ring.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 3729 as the face-on late-type host of AT 2018hso and adopts a 21.26 Mpc distance after comparing Tully-Fisher and kinematic distance estimates.

quotes.quote: Its coordinates are RA= 11^ h 33^ m 51.96^ s , Dec= +53 07 07.10 [ J2000 ] , 24.9 south, 23.7 east of the core of the face-on, late-type galaxy NGC 3729. The distances to NGC 3729 reported in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED NED: http://nedwww.ipac.caltech.edu/ ) are based on the Tully-Fisher method e.g., Willick1997ApJS..109..333W, Tully2009AJ....138..323T , and range from 21.10 to 21.88 Mpc. The kinematic distance corrected for Virgo Infall and obtained adopting a standard cosmology ( H_ 0 =73 Mpc ^ 1 , _ M =0.27, _ =0.73) is d_ k =20.80 1.5 Mpc Mould2000ApJ...545..547M , and is in good agreement with Tully-Fisher estimates. Hence, hereafter, we will adopt as distance to NGC 3729 the weighted average of the above values, d=21.26 0.56 Mpc, which provides a distance modulus = 31.64 0.06 mag .

gmw_00005767.name: 2CXO J215141.1-055049
gmw_00005767.overview: 2CXO J215141.1-055049 is an active galactic nucleus located at an estimated redshift of approximately 2 [arXiv:2203.13825]. It is identified as a possible Compton-thick candidate characterized by high obscuration, a hard X-ray spectrum, and high X-ray luminosity [arXiv:2203.13825].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2CXO J215141.1-055049 as a possible Compton-thick AGN candidate with high obscuration, high X-ray luminosity, hard spectrum, z about 2, and no cross-matched counterpart.

quotes.quote: In a particularly notable case, we have identified a possible Compton-thick candidate (2CXO J215141.1-055049). A Compton-thick AGN is defined as N_H 1.5 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 = _T^ 1 , where _T is the Thomson cross-section. This definition corresponds to log N_H 24.2 , and for the source in question, the best-fit log N_H 24.0 nearly reaches that threshold, with the 1 error range extending beyond log N_H = 24.2 , thus demanding consideration as a Compton-thick candidate.

The spectrum of the Compton-thick candidate fitted with the XZ AGN model is shown Figure fig:all_CT , and a breakdown of the source's characteristics is given in Table tab:CT_catalog showing, among other quantities, its high 2--10 keV X-ray luminosity (log L_X 46 ) and considerable CSC-documented hardness ratio (HR 0.999 ). As mentioned, the 1 error range on its best-fit log N_H 24.0 includes values beyond the Compton-thick threshold, and XZ found a best-fit z 2 . This redshift estimate is assigned a high probability of accuracy by the machine learning algorithm ( P_+ 0.99 ), which suggests it has a 99 chance of meeting our mild- to top-tier success thresholds.

gmw_00005768.name: PRG 4
gmw_00005768.overview: PRG 4 is a polar ring galaxy featuring a nearly edge-on disc with well-defined thin edges [arXiv:1202.5033]. The system contains two distinct kinematic components, where ionized gas rotates in a plane strongly inclined to the inner stellar body [arXiv:1107.1966].

sources.summary: Spectroscopy of SPRC-10 reveals two kinematic components: gas rotating in a plane strongly inclined to the inner stellar body and stellar rotation in the central galaxy.

quotes.quote: SPRC-10 Two kinematic components are clearly detected here. The distribution of line-of-sight velocities corresponds to that, expected for a galaxy, in which all the ionized gas rotates in the plane, strongly inclined to the inner stellar body. Along the first section with PA(1)=39 (the major axis of the ring) a significant gradient of line-of-sight velocities of ionized gas is observed, while the line-of-sight velocity of stars in the inner region (<3 arcsec) barely varies. In the slit position along the major axis of the central galaxy ( PA(2)=138 ) the situation is reversed -- here we find a zero gradient of line-of-sight velocities of gas, and a substantial gradient for the stellar component, where a symmetrical rotation curve can clearly be traced. Interestingly, in the slit position with PA(1)=39 at >3-4 arcsec on both sides of the nucleus we can see a symmetrical increase in line-of-sight velocities of stars, in the same direction with the ionized gas in the ring.

sources.summary: PRG 4 shows a nearly edge-on polar disc, a slight host-axis offset, possible warping or inclination effects, and extended line emission from the ring.

quotes.quote: PRG 4 (SDSS J082038.18+153659.7): The image of the galaxy shows the presence of a nearly edge-on disc with well-defined thin edges. The angle between the polar disc and the minor axis of the host is about 5 ^ . Closely inspecting the system reveals that the photocentre, defined by isophotes several arcsec from the nucleus, does not coincide with the the peak brightness location. This might be due to the apparent warping of the polar disc or due to an inclination effect. The continuum-subtracted H + [ NII ] image reveals extended emission from the north-east part of the ring.

gmw_00005769.name: PRG 5
gmw_00005769.overview: PRG 5 is a ring galaxy with an outer ring tilted by approximately 20 degrees relative to the polar axis of its host galaxy [arXiv:1202.5033]. It is the most luminous member of a close group containing at least four early-type galaxies [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 5 has an outer ring tilted from the host polar axis, possible background red light near the ring, and membership in a close early-type group.

quotes.quote: PRG 5 (SDSS J084832.00+322012.4): The appearance of the outer ring resembles to some extent that of PRG 4. The ring is tilted by about 20 ^ with respect to the polar axis of the host galaxy, which is rounder than the main body of PRG 4. Diffuse light is detected close to the edge of the ring about 15 arcsec to the south-west. However, this red feature does not seem distorted and is possibly a background early-type galaxy. PRG 5 is the most luminous member of a close group of at least four galaxies, whose spectra are characteristic of typical early-type galaxies.

gmw_00005770.name: PRG 6
gmw_00005770.overview: PRG 6 is a disturbed galaxy merger remnant that exhibits asymmetrically deformed outer isophotes [arXiv:1202.5033]. Its structure features a bright condensation, a sharp-edged arclet, two thick elongated features, and a small distorted object surrounded by diffuse material [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 6 is presented as a disturbed merger remnant with asymmetric host isophotes, condensations, arclets, long features, and diffuse material around a small distorted object.

quotes.quote: PRG 6 (SDSS J091453.64+493824.0): This galaxy reveals a wealth of morphological detail characteristic of a disturbed merger remnant. The outer isophotes of the main body are asymmetrically deformed and the surface brightness drops steeply towards north-east and south-west. A bright condensation is located 10 arcsec away from the centre of the galaxy at the west part of the ring and a sharp-edge arclet emerges at the east part of the ring about 18 arcsec from the nucleus. Two thick elongated features extend more than 40 arcsec to the west and twice that distance to the east at a position angle that is intermediate between that of the host and the polar ring. Furthermore, a small distorted object, is located about 20 arcsec north of the centre within a diffuse fan of faint material.

gmw_00005771.name: PRG 7
gmw_00005771.overview: PRG 7 is a polar ring galaxy featuring an elliptical body crossed by an asymmetrical stellar disc tilted 15 degrees from the polar axis [arXiv:1202.5033]. While one side of the ring is relatively relaxed, the opposite side exhibits diffuse light stretching outward [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 7 contains an elliptical body crossed by an asymmetrical tilted stellar disc, diffuse extension on one side of the ring, and a relatively relaxed opposite side.

quotes.quote: PRG 7 (SDSS J094207.34+362417.2): The elliptical body is crossed by an asymmetrical stellar disc tilted 15 ^ from the polar axis. Although the north-east part of the ring seems to end with a faint light enhancement, a careful inspection reveals diffuse light stretching farther away at this direction. The opposite side of the ring is relatively relaxed. Two galaxies, a bluish star-forming galaxy and an early-type galaxy, are found at projected distances of about 1 Mpc from PRG 7.

gmw_00005772.name: PRG 8
gmw_00005772.overview: PRG 8 is a lenticular host galaxy surrounded by a fragmented, highly inclined ring structure [arXiv:1202.5033]. This ring extends more than twice the radius of the host and features a faint, thick tail stretching to the south-east [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 8 is interpreted from deep images as a fragmented, highly inclined ring around an S0 host, with a faint tail and increasingly eccentric outer isophotes.

quotes.quote: PRG 8 (SDSS J094302.33-004850.0): At a first glance, the S0 host appears on the single SDSS frames to be surrounded by a warped disc. Our deep images show that this structure is probably a fragmented and highly inclined ring. The ring extends more than twice the radius of the host, but has a faint thick tail stretching about 40 arcsec to the south-east. The isophotes of the main body become more eccentric at larger radii, implying a more substantial disc than for other hosts in our sample. NED lists four reddish galaxies with known redshift near PRG 8, the closest one at a projected distance of about 0.3 Mpc.

gmw_00005773.name: PRG 9
gmw_00005773.overview: PRG 9 is a galaxy characterized by isophotes disturbed by internal dust extinction and a faint, asymmetric polar structure [arXiv:1202.5033]. The galaxy exhibits line emission in its central regions and along this polar structure, with fiber observations detecting strong emission lines characteristic of a starburst [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 9 has dust-disturbed isophotes, a faint asymmetric polar structure, line emission in the center and polar component, and starburst-like SDSS fiber emission.

quotes.quote: PRG 9 (SDSS J104623.65+063710.2): The isophotes of the galaxy are disturbed by internal dust extinction. A faint asymmetric structure crosses the galaxy 25 ^ from the minor axis and is almost hidden by the outer isophotes of the main body. Our continuum-subtracted H + [ NII ] image reveals significant emission in the central parts of the galaxy and along the polar structure. The SDSS fiber also detected strong emission lines characteristic of starburst galaxies. The environment is rich in galaxies, several of them 2-3 mag brighter than PRG 8.

gmw_00005774.name: PRG 10
gmw_00005774.overview: PRG 10 is a polar-ring galaxy candidate featuring two bright, well-defined perpendicular components [arXiv:1202.5033]. Its slightly warped ring extends far beyond the host galaxy, exhibiting a weak dust lane and extended line emission that indicates recent star formation [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 10 is the paper's best polar-ring candidate, with bright perpendicular components, a warped extended ring, a weak dust lane, and extended line emission along the ring.

quotes.quote: PRG 10 (SDSS J114444.02+230944.9): This is the best candidate of a polar ring galaxy in our sample since the two perpendicular components are bright and well-defined. The ring extends far beyond the host and is slightly warped at the edges. Our deep images reveal a weak dust lane following the ring at the north-west side of the host and a faint diffuse halo surrounding the north-east edge of the ring. The continuum-subtracted H + [ NII ] image shows extended emission along the ring, indicating the presence of recently formed stellar population. The light in the inner 3 arcsec of the galaxy does not show clear signs of star formation and is characteristic of early-type galaxies.

gmw_00005775.name: PRG 11
gmw_00005775.overview: PRG 11 is a galaxy featuring a faint thick disc and broad fans of diffuse light [arXiv:1202.5033]. Its spectrum is classified as composite, indicating that both star formation and nuclear activity contribute significantly to the system [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 11 has a faint thick disc, broad diffuse fans, an uncertain north-west feature, composite emission-line classification, and evidence for both star formation and nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: PRG 11 (SDSS J115228.29+050044.7): The faint thick disc, which crosses the main body at 5 ^ from polar axis, is very similar to that of PRG 3. Our deep images show broad fans of diffuse light around the galaxy, and an elongated feature 15 arcsec from nucleus on the north-west side of the ring. However, we cannot determine whether this is stellar debris gravitationally bound to the system or light from a background source. The SDSS spectrum shows strong emission lines but the analysis of their nature is not decisive, and the object is classified `composite' in the MPA/JHU catalogue. The combination of strong narrow components and a weak broad H component implies that both star formation and nuclear activity contribute significantly to the spectrum (Wang Wei 2008).

gmw_00005776.name: PRG 12
gmw_00005776.overview: PRG 12 is a ring galaxy with an inclined disc extending to about three times the host radius [arXiv:1202.5033]. The galaxy features a twisted north-east ring edge, weak host emission, and at least 15 neighboring galaxies within 2 Mpc [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: The paper reports that SPRC-39 resembles SPRC-10 and SPRC-14, with central stellar velocities nearly constant, ring stellar motion at large radii, and asymmetric gas rotation.

quotes.quote: SPRC-39 Here we managed to obtain only one section -- along the major axis of the ring. Nonetheless, the observed picture is very similar to what we have seen in SPRC-10 and SPRC-14. Namely, the line-of-sight velocities of stars barely vary in the centre of ( <6-8 arcsec), since the slit passes near the minor axis of the central disc. At large distances, we can see the movement of stars in the ring. We note here a strong asymmetry of the rotation curves of gas in both directions from the centre -- in the SW part it continues to grow, while to the NE from the centre it achieves plateau.

sources.summary: PRG 12 has an inclined disc extending to about three host radii, dust-obscured host isophotes, a twisted ring edge, weak host emission, and many nearby galaxies.

quotes.quote: PRG 12 (SDSS J130816.92+452235.1): The disc is inclined 30 ^ to the minor axis of the host, extends to about three times the radius of the host and exhibits internal dust obscuration which slightly distorts the outer isophotes of the host. The north-east edge of the ring is twisted by 45 ^ to the east. Our continuum-subtracted H image shows some emission from the host, but this is barely, if at all, seen in the SDSS spectrum. We count at least 15 galaxies within 2 Mpc around PRG 12. The brightest galaxy in this neighbourhood lies only one arcmin (about 40 kpc in projected distance) from PRG 12, but the two galaxies do not show clear signs of interaction.

gmw_00005777.name: PRG 13
gmw_00005777.overview: PRG 13 is a polar-ring galaxy featuring a highly inclined, warped main disc with a dust lane and an asymmetric polar ring containing luminous blue clumps [arXiv:1202.5033]. This polar structure is weaker than its host galaxy and exhibits a double-horn profile [arXiv:1302.7273].

sources.summary: The paper treats SPRC-47 as a CO-detected polar-ring system with a double-horn profile, broad Tully-Fisher velocity offset, weaker polar structure than its host, and a very flattened dark-matter system.

quotes.quote: Two of the PRGs, P47 and P48, clearly show double-horn profiles, indicative of rotating disks or rings.

In Fig. fig:TF , one of the PRGs falls in the expected range for a spherical potential (SPRC-14), two are mildly offset (SPRC-69 and SPRC-42), and two are very far from the usual relation, with very broad velocities (SPRC-48 and SPRC-47). This large scatter might indicate different formation mechanisms for the systems, and at least four out of the five should have their dark matter halo aligned with the polar plane. The position of the five PRGs in the TF diagram does not appear related to the mass ratio between the polar disk/ring and the host. The galaxies SPRC-42 and SPRC-69 have polar structures brighter than their hosts, while SPRC-14 and SPRC-48 are comparable, and SPRC-47 is weaker than its host.
There is no obvious relation with the PR spatial extent either. The two most extreme cases (SPRC-47 and SPRC-48) must have a very flattened dark-matter system, as flat as a disk.

sources.summary: PRG 13 is described as unusual, with a highly inclined warped main disc, an asymmetric polar ring with blue clumps, and a very faint warped outer structure.

quotes.quote: PRG 13 (SDSS J135941.70+250046.0): The main body of this galaxy is unusual. The high eccentricity, prominent dust lane along the major axis and the apparent warped structure indicate that this is a highly-inclined disc warped on both sides. Our deep images reveal an asymmetric polar ring 41 arcsec wide, tilted 15 ^ from the minor axis. The ring extends far beyond the main body and contains several luminous blue clumps. A warped diffuse outer structure extends beyond the ring and is too faint to be seen in single SDSS exposures.

gmw_00005778.name: PRG 14
gmw_00005778.overview: PRG 14 is an early-type galaxy that belongs to a compact group [arXiv:1202.5033] and hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1202.5033]. It features an inclined bluish ring, which deep imaging suggests may be a helical structure or diffuse stellar fans [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 14 shows a bluish inclined ring or possible helical/fan-like structure, hosts an AGN, and belongs to a compact group of early-type galaxies.

quotes.quote: PRG 14 (SDSS J151114.08+370237.6): The SDSS colour images (see Moiseev et al. \ 2011) show an inclined bluish ring around the central body. However, our deep images reveal a more complex structure where the surrounding material appears to form more than one complete turn around the galaxy, as in NGC 2685. Note that what we interpret as an helical structure around the host might actually be diffuse stellar fans on both sides of the main body. PRG 14 host an AGN and is a member of a compact group of several early-type galaxies.

gmw_00005779.name: 9C J1503+4528
gmw_00005779.overview: 9C J1503+4528 is a compact steep-spectrum radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.521 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509128]. It is a young radio source with an estimated age of 10^4 years, likely triggered by a minor interaction [arXiv:astro-ph/0605460].

sources.summary: The paper describes 9C J1503+4528 as a very young compact FRII radio galaxy whose shocks, young stellar population, and AGN triggering likely reflect a minor interaction.

quotes.quote: Draft version 31 Mar 2005 firstpage - lastpage 2005 9C J1503+4528 is a very young CSS radio galaxy, with an age of order 10^ 4 years. This source is therefore an ideal laboratory for the study of the intrinsic host galaxy/IGM properties, interactions between the radio source and surrounding ISM, links between star formation and AGN activity and the radio source triggering mechanism. Here we present the results of a spectroscopic analysis of this source, considering each of these aspects of radio source physics. We find that shock ionization by the young radio source is important in the central regions of the galaxy on scales similar to that of the radio source itself, whilst evidence for an AGN ionization cone is observed at greater distances. Line and continuum features require the presence of a young stellar population, the best-fit model for which implies an age of 5 10^6 years, significantly older than the radio source.

If both the triggering of the radio source activity and the formation of the young stellar population had the same underlying cause, this source provides a sequence for the events surrounding the triggering process. We propose that the AGN activity in 9C J1503+4528 was caused by a relatively minor interaction, and that a super-massive black hole powering the radio jets must have been in place before the AGN was triggered.

sources.summary: The paper presents 9C J1503+4528 as a very young compact steep-spectrum radio galaxy, using Keck spectra to study its size, redshift, emission-line structure, and radio-source triggering.

quotes.quote: 9C J1503+4528 is a very young CSS radio galaxy, with an age of order 10^ 4 years. This source is an ideal laboratory for the study of the intrinsic host galaxy/IGM properties, radio source interactions, evidence for young stellar populations and the radio source triggering mechanism.

9C J1503+4528 is a classical double CSS source at z = 0.521 , with an angular size of 0.5^ , corresponding to a projected linear size of 3 kpc. Values for the cosmological parameters of _0 = 0.27 , _ = 0.73 and H_0 = 65 km s ^ 1 are assumed throughout. Spectra of 9C J1503+4528 were obtained using Keck LRIS (Oke et al 1995) with a 1 ^ slit aligned both parallel and perpendicular to the radio source axis, and integration times of 1800s for each position angle.

Determining the systemic redshift of 9C J1503+4528 was complicated by the inherent structure in the emission lines: the redshift implied by the peak of the line emission on the continuum centroid differs substantially from that of the narrow extended component. However, the various absorption features in the spectra (Ca H K, high-order Balmer lines) all suggest a redshift identical to that determined from the extended narrow component, giving a value of z = 0.521 .

gmw_00005780.name: SDSS J1231 + 0138
gmw_00005780.overview: SDSS J1231 + 0138 is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0902.1366, arXiv:1805.03888] classified as a radio-intermediate weak-line quasar [arXiv:0902.1366]. It is also identified as a BL Lacertae candidate with an intrinsically featureless spectrum [arXiv:1805.03888].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1231 + 0138 as the only WLQ with enough X-ray counts for spectral analysis, finding a typical radio-intermediate AGN photon index and no significant intrinsic absorption.

quotes.quote: table 3 The X-ray Spectrum of SDSS J1231 + 0138 SDSS J1231 + 0138 is the only WLQ in our sample with sufficient \ counts to allow an investigation of its \ spectrum. The spectrum of the source was extracted with the ciao task psextract using a circular region of 2 \ radius centered on the \ centroid. The background was extracted from a source-free annulus with inner and outer radii of 5 \ and 25 , respectively. The spectrum was binned in groups of 10 counts per bin. We used xspec v11.3.2 (Arnaud 1996) to fit the spectrum with a power-law model and a Galactic-absorption component, which was kept fixed during the fit ( =1.81 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 ; Dickey Lockman 1990).

The best-fit photon index is =1.76^ +0.35 _ 0.34 with ^2=5.57 for 8 degrees of freedom; this photon index is consistent with the value obtained from the band ratio (Table  counts ) and is typical for radio-intermediate AGNs such as SDSS J1231 + 0138 ( =-0.32 ; see basic ; e.g., Reeves Turner 2000). Adding an intrinsic-absorption component at the redshift of the source did not improve the fit; the upper limit on the intrinsic absorption is 5.70 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 (at 90 confidence). Mean X-ray Spectral Properties of High-Redshift WLQs Since all of our WLQs, except for SDSS J1231 + 0138 (see sdssj1231 ), lack sufficient \ counts for individual spectral analyses, we have obtained mean \ spectral constraints for high-redshift WLQs as a group by jointly fitting their \ spectra (e.g., Vignali 2005; S06).

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J123132.37+013814.1 as a high-redshift BL Lac candidate with a Ly-forest lower limit and no intrinsic emission lines in deeper GTC data.

quotes.quote: SDSS J123132.37+013814.1 : The lower limit of the redshift was set z 3.147 from the detection of the Ly- forest in the SDSS spectra. We confirm the detection of the Ly- forest plotkin10 at the same redshift while we do not measure any further emission line in the region 5200-9200 at (SNR = 30) and with EW 0.40 making this source, apart from line-of-sight intervening systems, intrinsically featureless.

gmw_00005781.name: SDSS J1302 + 0030
gmw_00005781.overview: SDSS J1302 + 0030 is a high-redshift, weak-line quasar [arXiv:0904.2181]. The galaxy is radio-undetected [arXiv:0904.2181] and exhibits weak X-ray emission [arXiv:0904.2181], which was detected only after combining multiple Chandra exposures [arXiv:0902.1366].

sources.summary: The paper observes SDSSJ1302 as a z>4 weak-line quasar, finding no radio detection, weak X-rays, a poorer SED fit, and new infrared, optical, polarimetric, and VLA data.

quotes.quote: Sections sec:ir , sec:optical , and sec:synchrotron then present results from an extensive observational campaign focusing on the WLQs SDSSJ1302, SDSSJ1408, SDSSJ1442, and SDSSJ1532. These data include Spitzer 3.6--24 m mid-infrared (mid-IR) photometry and near-IR photometry in J , H , and K_ s bands that are compared to the spectral energy distributions (SEDs) of typical quasars (Section sec:ir ); multiple epochs of optical spectroscopy and i band photometry (in addition to the available SDSS data) that constrain the line and continuum variability of WLQs (Section sec:optical ); and optical imaging polarimetry along with Very Large Array (VLA) radio continuum observations at L and C bands that test for the presence of synchrotron emission from a relativistic jet (Section sec:synchrotron ).

These sources were selected early on in the survey to be representative of the handful of WLQs known at the time; their emission-line strengths range from undetectable (e.g., SDSSJ1532) to weak, but clearly detected (e.g., SDSSJ1442), and two are FIRST radio sources (SDSSJ1408, R=14 ; SDSSJ1442, R=26 ), while the other two have no radio detections. The FIRST-detected sources also have strong Chandra X-ray detections SDSSJ1408, _ ox =-1.54 ; SDSSJ1442, _ ox =-1.42 shem06 , while SDSSJ1302 and SDSSJ1532 are X-ray weak _ ox =-2.08 and _ ox <-1.90 , respectively; shem06,shem09 . This section presents Spitzer mid-IR photometry in the 3.6--24 m range (Section sec:spitzer ) and near-IR photometry in J , H , and K_ s band (Section sec:nir ), which we use to construct rest-frame 0.1--5 m SEDs of WLQs that can be compared to those of normal quasars (Section sec:seds ).

This model fits the SEDs of the radio-detected quasars better ( ^2/ dof =1.7 for SDSSJ1408 and 1.3 for SDSSJ1442) than it does the SEDs of the radio-undetected quasars ( ^2/ dof =4.7 for SDSSJ1302 and 5.6 for SDSSJ1532). The radio-detected sources are also both bluer and have higher temperature dust, which is consistent with their redder [ 5.8 ] [ 8.0 ] colors.

sources.summary: The paper reports that SDSS J1302 + 0030 was undetected in an earlier Chandra exposure but detected after combining Cycle 8 and Cycle 4 data, entering the joint WLQ X-ray analysis.

quotes.quote: These sources were selected from the SDSS archive for being the brightest sources of this class; except for SDSS J1302 + 0030, these were not targeted in X-rays prior to Cycle 8. SDSS J1302 + 0030 was not detected in a 10.81 ks \ Cycle 4 exposure; our additional 10.50 ks exposure in Cycle 8 has enabled its detection (see below).

Except for SDSS J1302 + 0030, all of the targeted WLQs were detected with 3--110 full-band counts. A significant detection of SDSS J1302 + 0030 (using wavdetect with a false-positive probability threshold of 10 ^ 4 ) was achieved only by merging the Cycle 8 exposure (with 3 full-band counts) with that from Cycle 4 (with 2 full-band counts; see S06); the \ counts for this source, presented in Table  counts , were obtained from the merged image.

Eleven sources with \ spectra were included in the joint-fitting process, including the nine WLQs targeted in this work (i.e., all sources from Tables  log and  counts including the merged dataset of SDSS J1302 + 0030) and the only other two detected WLQs from S06, namely SDSS J140850.91 + 020522.7 and SDSS J144231.72 + 011055.2 (see Tables 1--3 of S06).

gmw_00005782.name: SDSS J1012 + 5313
gmw_00005782.overview: SDSS J1012 + 5313 is a newly identified weak emission-line quasar located at a redshift of z=3.0 [arXiv:0902.1366]. The active galactic nucleus was serendipitously detected in X-ray observations using the MOS1 and MOS2 detectors [arXiv:0902.1366].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J1012 + 5313 as a serendipitously observed WLQ, detected in XMM MOS images during flaring background and later excluded from strong joint spectral leverage.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1012 + 5313 at z=3.0 is a newly identified WLQ from SDSS Data Release 5 (Schneider 2007).

The SDSS J1012 + 5313 observation included only MOS1 and MOS2 images, and the entire observation was carried out during a period of flaring activity; hence no time filtering was employed. The SDSS J1237 + 6301 observation was filtered in time to remove periods of background flaring in which the count rates of each MOS (pn) detector exceeded 0.35 (1.0) counts s ^ 1 for events having E>10 keV; the net exposure times were 10.7 ks and 4.9 ks for the MOS1/MOS2 and pn detectors, respectively. We detected SDSS J1012 + 5313 at 7.0 \ from the \ aimpoint in both the MOS1 and MOS2 detectors.

We note that there are only two other detected WLQs with \ spectra: SDSS J1012 + 5313 (see xmm_obs ) and SDSS J004054.65 091526.8 (S06); these spectra do not provide a significant contribution to the spectral constraints since the first was taken during a period of flaring activity ( xmm_obs ) and the second was detected at low signal-to-noise (with only 31.8 11.6 net source counts; Schneider 2003).

gmw_00005783.name: A
gmw_00005783.overview: Galaxy A is an underluminous disk galaxy with an emission-line spectrum consistent with normal star-forming galaxies [arXiv:0905.1327]. It resides in a dark matter halo of approximately 10^11 h^-1 solar masses and likely shares a common halo with its neighbor, galaxy B [arXiv:0905.1327].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy A as the closest absorber-neighboring underluminous disk galaxy, gives its projected separation, star-forming spectrum, and halo mass/radius in the HE0226 4110 field.

quotes.quote: Table 1 shows that the galaxy with the smallest projected distance from the QSO ( A ) is an underluminous disk galaxy of luminosity 0.05 L_ * (adopting M_ R_* 5 h=-20.4 from Blanton et al. \ 2003). The projected distance between A and HE0226 4110 is =26.5 h^ 1 physical kpc.

Galaxies A and B have emission-line spectra consistent with normal star-forming galaxies, while C exhibits absorption dominated spectral features that indicates a more evolved underlying stellar population.

\ M_R relation of Tinker Conroy (2009), we estimate that galaxies A , B , and C reside in halos of M_h=10^ 11 , 10^ 11.5 , and 10^ 11.5 \ h^ 1 M_ , respectively. The corresponding halo radii R_h R_ 200 We adopt R_ 200 to represent the size of a dark matter halo, which corresponds to the radius within which the enclosed mean density is 200 times the background matter density. are R_h=93 , 137, and 137 \ h^ 1 kpc for A , B , C , respectively. The expected halo radii of these galaxies together with the projected separations between one another indicate that galaxies B and C do not share a common halo, whereas galaxies A and B (separated by 50 \ h^ 1 projected physical kpc and 300 ) are likely to share a common halo (based on the two-point correlation function, the probability of having two unrelated galaxies at this separation is 0.5 ; z05 ).

gmw_00005784.name: B
gmw_00005784.overview: Galaxy B is a luminous disk galaxy [arXiv:0905.1327]. It exhibits emission-line spectra consistent with a normal star-forming galaxy and is likely to share a common dark matter halo with galaxy A [arXiv:0905.1327].

sources.summary: The paper describes galaxy B as a luminous disk galaxy near the NeVIII absorber, with star-forming emission lines, a 76.5 h^-1 kpc impact parameter, and a halo likely shared with A.

quotes.quote: The two other objects are more luminous disk galaxies of 0.25 L_ * . These galaxies have impact parameters of =76.5 \ h^ 1 physical kpc ( B ) and =197.3 \ h^ 1 physical kpc ( C ), respectively. Galaxies A and B have emission-line spectra consistent with normal star-forming galaxies, while C exhibits absorption dominated spectral features that indicates a more evolved underlying stellar population.

\ M_R relation of Tinker Conroy (2009), we estimate that galaxies A , B , and C reside in halos of M_h=10^ 11 , 10^ 11.5 , and 10^ 11.5 \ h^ 1 M_ , respectively. The corresponding halo radii R_h R_ 200 We adopt R_ 200 to represent the size of a dark matter halo, which corresponds to the radius within which the enclosed mean density is 200 times the background matter density. are R_h=93 , 137, and 137 \ h^ 1 kpc for A , B , C , respectively. The expected halo radii of these galaxies together with the projected separations between one another indicate that galaxies B and C do not share a common halo, whereas galaxies A and B (separated by 50 \ h^ 1 projected physical kpc and 300 ) are likely to share a common halo (based on the two-point correlation function, the probability of having two unrelated galaxies at this separation is 0.5 ; z05 ). Analogous to the Local Group, it is probably more appropriate to consider B and C as two separate halos, instead of a single virialed group \ .

gmw_00005785.name: C
gmw_00005785.overview: Galaxy C is a luminous disk galaxy [arXiv:0905.1327]. It exhibits absorption-dominated spectral features that indicate an evolved underlying stellar population, and it resides in its own separate dark matter halo [arXiv:0905.1327].

sources.summary: The paper describes galaxy C as a luminous disk galaxy near the absorber with the largest impact parameter, absorption-dominated spectral features, and a separate halo from galaxy B.

quotes.quote: The two other objects are more luminous disk galaxies of 0.25 L_ * . These galaxies have impact parameters of =76.5 \ h^ 1 physical kpc ( B ) and =197.3 \ h^ 1 physical kpc ( C ), respectively. Galaxies A and B have emission-line spectra consistent with normal star-forming galaxies, while C exhibits absorption dominated spectral features that indicates a more evolved underlying stellar population.

The corresponding halo radii R_h R_ 200 We adopt R_ 200 to represent the size of a dark matter halo, which corresponds to the radius within which the enclosed mean density is 200 times the background matter density. are R_h=93 , 137, and 137 \ h^ 1 kpc for A , B , C , respectively. Analogous to the Local Group, it is probably more appropriate to consider B and C as two separate halos, instead of a single virialed group \ .

gmw_00005786.name: S5-z17-1
gmw_00005786.overview: S5-z17-1 is a very luminous high-redshift galaxy candidate identified in the Stephan's Quintet field [arXiv:2211.03896, arXiv:2208.01612]. ALMA observations did not detect a dust continuum, but they revealed an emission line feature that may represent oxygen emission at redshift 16.01 or carbon emission at redshift 4.61 [arXiv:2211.03896, arXiv:2208.01612].

sources.summary: The paper presents ALMA observations of S5-z17-1, finding no dust continuum, a tentative line, and a favored very luminous high-redshift solution with remaining lower-redshift alternatives.

quotes.quote: We present ALMA Band 7 observations of a remarkably bright galaxy candidate at z_ phot = 16.7^ +1.9 _ 0.3 ( M_ UV = 21.6 ), S5-z17-1, identified in \ Early Release Observation data of Stephen's Quintet. We do not detect the dust continuum at 866 m, ruling out the possibility that \ is a low- z dusty starburst with a star-formation rate of 30 M_ yr ^ 1 . We detect a 5.1 line feature at 338.726 0.007 GHz exactly coinciding with the \ source position, with a 2 likelihood of the signal being spurious.

The most likely line identification would be 52 m at z=16.01 or 158 m at z=4.61 , whose line luminosities do not violate the non-detection of the dust continuum in both cases. Together with three other z 11--13 candidate galaxies recently observed with ALMA, we conduct a joint ALMA and \ spectral energy distribution (SED) analysis and find that the high- z solution at z 11--17 is favored in every candidate as a very blue (UV continuum slope of 2.3 ) and luminous ( M_ UV 24: 21 ) system.

This UV luminosity is comparable to that of GN-z11 oesch2016 , making S5-z17-1 the second most luminous object at z>11 after HD1 ( M_ UV =-23.6 ). Such an identification in the small survey volume among the early \ observations could present a challenge to the current models of early galaxy formation and potentially even the underlying CDM cosmological framework e.g., steinhardt2016, steinhardt2022, mason2022, boylan2022, lovell2022, menci2022 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies S5-z16-1 as a possible z about 16 Stephan's Quintet-field candidate with robust colors/non-detections, an ALMA line, and unexpectedly high luminosity.

quotes.quote: The other candidate at z 17 is S5-z16-1 at z=16.41^ +0.66 _ 0.55 identified in the Stephan's quintet field. Although this source is located in a region whose exposure time is relatively short compared to the central region of the field, our position-dependent estimates of flux uncertainties indicate that the source detection, color, and non-detections are robust against the uncertainties. An emission line feature is detected with ALMA in S5-z16-1 2022arXiv221103896F , which would be interpreted as either Oiii 52 m at z=16.01 or Cii 1 58 m at z=4.61 . We include this possible candidate in the luminosity function calculation, although the luminosity is remarkably high compared to our expectations at this high redshift.

gmw_00005787.name: NGC 5238
gmw_00005787.overview: NGC 5238 is a gas-rich dwarf irregular galaxy located at the periphery of the Canes Venatici I group [arXiv:2405.12284]. It is relatively isolated [arXiv:2405.12284], but it displays a distorted disc and low-surface-brightness structures likely caused by interaction with a smaller satellite [arXiv:2405.12284].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 5238 as an isolated gas-rich dwarf irregular whose low-surface-brightness structures and distorted disc are reproduced by interaction with a smaller satellite.

quotes.quote: NGC 5238 (also known as UGC 8565, MRK 1479, and IZw 64) is a gas-rich (2.8-2.9) 10^7 , Cannon2016,Calzetti2015 dwarf irregular galaxy at the periphery of the Canes Venatici I group, at a distance of D=4.51 0.04 Tully2009 from the Earth. According to its low tidal index =-0.4 (a quantifiable metric of the density contrast from massive neighboring galaxies; Karachentsev2013 ), NGC 5238 is fairly isolated, more than 1 from any known massive galaxy. NGC 5238 was observed in the context of several surveys, such as the H 11HUGS survey Kennicutt2008 , the GALEX ultraviolet (UV) imaging survey Lee2011 , the infrared Spitzer Local Volume Legacy (LVL) survey Dale2009 , the HST optical ANGST survey Dalcanton2009 and the HST UV LEGUS survey Calzetti2015 .

Indeed, in the case of NGC 5238, the ``umbrella''-like feature detected in the deep LBT images coincides with a similar structure in the RGB count map, indicating that it cannot be accounted for by recent and offset bursts of star formation, but that it can be either the remnant of an accreted companion or a distortion of NGC 5238 of external origin. More in general, we note that the old stellar halo of NGC 5238 extends for a diameter of 360 , or 8 , well beyond the outer displayed 1 ^ 2 contour.

After 0.67 , the satellite galaxy has entirely crossed NGC 5238 once, and this passage is indeed highly disruptive for both the satellite system and the host. On NGC 5238 the gravitational effect is such to significantly distort its disc. The satellite, instead, loses coherence to the point that its surface brightness reaches the detection threshold, making it extremely faint.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5238 as showing one of the strongest outer luminosity excesses, low-level shape changes, and color-profile flattening similar to DDO 168.

quotes.quote: Some of our galaxies show a luminosity excess above a pure exponential in the surface brightness profiles at large radii (Fig. profiles ), most strongly in NGC 5238, but also in DDO 168, DDO 169, DDO 183, NGC 5477, DDO 190, and DDO 194.

Galaxies such as DDO 168, DDO 169 and NGC 5238 show such a behaviour, but at very low levels. For instance DDO 168 has an apparent ellipticity of = 0.60 and a position angle of 58 degrees for an ellipse fit at _B = 25 mag / and at _B = 27 mag / these values are resp. \ 0.42 and 62 . For DDO 169 these values are resp. \ 0.68 , 44 , 0.70 and 49 and for NGC 5238 these values are resp. \ 0.32 , 87 , 0.33 and 88 .

NGC 5238 : shows a relatively large excess in the surface brightness profiles, as well as a flattening of its colour profile. The characteristics of the excess and the colour profile are similar to those of DDO 168.

gmw_00005788.name: UGC 8760
gmw_00005788.overview: UGC 8760 is a fairly isolated dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:2405.12284] that exhibits a high rate of cluster formation [arXiv:1203.4826]. It features a stellar overdensity of red giant branch stars in its northeast region, which likely originated from a tangential interaction with a small satellite galaxy [arXiv:2405.12284].

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 8760 as an isolated dwarf irregular with a northeast stellar overdensity, reproduced by a tangential interaction with a small satellite galaxy.

quotes.quote: The dwarf irregular UGC 8760 (or DDO 183), at a distance of D=3.2 0.1 Dalcanton2009,Tully2009,Tully2013 , is another fairly isolated galaxy Karachentsev2013 and resides in the outer zone of the Ophiuchus–Sagittarius–Capricornus void Pustilnik2019 . As for NGC 5238, multi-wavelength data of UGC 8760 are available from various surveys (e.g., LVL, 11HUGS, ANGST, VLA-ANGST, and FIGGS). Stellar mass estimates derived from different methods are in the range =(2-4) 10^7 Johnson2013,Cook2014 , while the mass from FIGGS and VLA-ANGST data is 2.6 10^7 Begum2008,Ott2012 .
Similar to NGC 5238, the elongated northeast feature detected in UGC 8760 coincides with a clear RGB star overdensity. However, no extended stellar halo is detected in this galaxy.

At t=0.69 the satellite galaxy is, in projection, approximately at the same distance as the old stellar overdensity observed in UGC 8760. In the simulation, the satellite is stripped of a considerable amount of dark matter in the outer regions.

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC8760 as a high cluster-formation outlier in the young age range, though stochastic simulations can reproduce some of its scatter.

quotes.quote: The high outliers in the _ SFR relationship range from galaxies with no overlap in the 1000 simulations for three galaxies (UGC5336, UGC4459) to high values in the < 10 Myr age range, and one (UGC5692) in the < 100 Myr age range. These galaxies fall noticeably above the simulations. None of the simulations for UGC5336 nor UGC4459 and less than 1 and 7 of the simulations for UGC8760 and UGC5692, respectively, produced values as high as the observed data points. This makes UGC5692 a 2 outlier and UGC8760 a 3 outlier.
Also, both UGC5692 and UGC8760 show significant scatter from the _ SFR relationship, but fall within the scatter seen in the SLUG simulations.

gmw_00005789.name: PHL 909
gmw_00005789.overview: PHL 909 is a radio-quiet quasar hosted by a normal E4 elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0808.0918]. The host galaxy has a companion located 12 arcseconds to the west and exhibits a low velocity dispersion of 167 km/s [arXiv:0808.0918].

sources.summary: The paper describes PHL 909 as a radio-quiet quasar in a normal E4 elliptical host with a western companion, low velocity dispersion, low stellar mass, [OII] emission, and intermediate-age spectrum.

quotes.quote: PHL 909 (0054+144) PH 909 (labeled 2 in our plots) is a radio-quiet quasar with M _ V = 24.1 and L _ 5 GHz =1.1x10 ^ 40 erg s ^ 1 . Unlike our other radio-quiet hosts, this one is a normal E4 elliptical galaxy with M _ V = 22.2 and has a companion 12 arcsec to the west. Extended emission toward this companion was noted by dunlop93 and gehren84 , but not by bahcall97 . This host galaxy has our smallest measured velocity dispersion (167 km s ^ 1 ) that is also at the low end of the Dasyra et al. PG QSO sample.

gmw_00005790.name: 4C 31.63
gmw_00005790.overview: The radio-loud quasar 4C 31.63 is hosted by a massive, first-rank elongated elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0808.0918] that shows optical signs of a post-merger and three nearby companions [arXiv:0808.0918, arXiv:1712.06314]. It displays a Fanaroff-Riley II radio morphology with double lobes [arXiv:1712.06314], a long radio jet [arXiv:1802.04714], and a variable millimeter spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0006315].

sources.summary: The paper says B2 2201+31A has flat radio spectra where extrapolation affects IR estimates, variable mm slopes, near-IR emission above the sub-mm extrapolation, and a thermal short-wavelength continuum.

quotes.quote: In most of the cases this parameter does not affect the presence and the strength of the remaining IR flux, but its energy value may be important in FSRQ (PKS 0135 247, PKS 0637 75, and B2 2201+31A), since these objects have flat radio spectra for which extrapolation up to IR frequencies is comparable to the IR fluxes.
For B2 2201+31A the flattest mm power law ( (1.0-0.87 mm) = 0.09) was measured on February 1989 ( ), and the steepest one was measured on September 14th, 1993 ( (2.0-1.3-1.1 mm) = 0.72).

B2 2201+31A was observed on September 15th, 1993 also in the near-IR (simultaneous sub-mm and near-IR data are indicated by large open circles in Fig. FigsedFSRQ e). The near-IR data are above the extrapolation of the sub-mm data, suggesting the presence of two different spectral components in these two wavelength ranges (see the analogous case of 3C 273 in Robson et al. (1986)). This hypothesis is also suggested by the constant emission observed up to 60 m. A non-thermal source is expected to vary more at higher frequencies, due to greater energy losses. All these considerations suggest the short wavelength continuum is dominated by a thermal component.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C 31.63 as a radio-loud quasar hosted by a first-rank elongated elliptical with companions, extreme Fundamental Plane position, very large radius, high mass, and an old stellar spectrum.

quotes.quote: 4C 31.63 (2201+315) 4C 31.63 (labeled 9 in our plots) is a radio-loud quasar at M _ V = 25.1 and L _ 5 GHz =1.9x10 ^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . It has the spectrum of a typical strong Fe II object. The host galaxy is one of the few objects in our larger sample, and the only object in this subsample, that is clearly a first-rank elliptical at M _ V = 23.8. This galaxy is classified as a smooth elongated elliptical from both optical HST images bahcall97,hamilton02 and ground-based near infrared AO images guyon06 with three apparent companions within 5 of the QSO.
